```mermaid
    participant DEV as Development Team
    participant QA as QA Team

    SH->>SE: Submit Change Request CR-007
    SE->>SE: Analyze Impact on existing REQs
    SE->>CCB: Present Impact Analysis
    CCB->>CCB: Review Cost Schedule Risk
    CCB-->>SE: Decision Approved Rejected Deferred
    alt Approved
        SE->>DEV: Update SRS REQ-012
        DEV->>QA: Update Test Case TC-012
        QA-->>SE: Confirm Test Validity
        SE->>SH: Notify Acceptance
    else Rejected
        SE->>SH: Explain Rejection with Rationale
    else Deferred
        SE->>SH: Log to Backlog for Future Release
    end
```

**Reading Note:** This sequence diagram is the *exemplar answer* for a 14-mark "Explain Requirements Management with a suitable example" question. It captures all the essential interactions without needing textual description.

## 4.6 Mermaid Block Diagram — Validation vs. Verification (Examiner's Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V_VAL["VALIDATION Are we building the RIGHT system"]
        Q1[Stakeholder Need] --> Q2[Was this the actual need]
    end

    subgraph V_VER["VERIFICATION Are we building the system RIGHT"]
        Q3[Documented Requirement] --> Q4[Does the design satisfy the requirement]
    end

    V_VAL --> SYS[Final System]
    V_VER --> SYS
```

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board has asked this distinction as a 2-mark, 3-mark, and even 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the **bold** phrases in the diagram — they are the official IEEE definitions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A is *compulsory* and *uniform across all students in a session*. Answers should be **1 to 1.5 pages** with diagrams wherever possible.

### Question 1: Define Requirements Engineering. List its five major activities. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Requirements Engineering** is the systematic process of *eliciting, analyzing, specifying, validating, and managing* the requirements of a system throughout its life cycle, ensuring that the final system satisfies the stakeholder needs within defined constraints.
>
> The **five major activities** are:
> 1. **Requirements Elicitation** — Discovering stakeholder needs.
> 2. **Requirements Analysis** — Refining, classifying, and prioritizing.
> 3. **Requirements Specification** — Documenting the SRS.
> 4. **Requirements Validation** — Ensuring correctness and completeness.
> 5. **Requirements Management** — Handling changes, traceability, and baselines.

*(Expected length: 8–10 lines, 1 small diagram = full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2: Differentiate between Validation and Verification in the context of Requirements Engineering. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Validation | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| **Question answered** | Are we building the *right* system? | Are we building the system *right*? |
| **Focus** | Stakeholder need vs. system capability | Documented requirement vs. design |
| **When** | Throughout RE phase | After design/implementation |
| **Techniques** | Reviews, prototyping, simulation | Inspection, testing, walkthrough |
| **Output** | Validated SRS | Verified design/code |

*(A neat two-column table = 3 full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B includes an **Internal Choice**: students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both questions below carry the same cognitive level and marks distribution. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A: Requirements Engineering for a Hospital Management System `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Requirements Engineering process in detail. Apply the **MoSCoW** prioritization method to a Hospital Management System (HMS) and identify at least **eight** requirements with proper justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a **complete Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** for the HMS using at least six requirements, linking each to its source stakeholder, design module, code module, and test case. Compute the overall traceability coverage. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Requirements Engineering Process + MoSCoW (7 marks)

The Requirements Engineering process consists of **five iterative activities**:

1. **Elicitation:** Conduct interviews with doctors, nurses, patients, pharmacists, and the billing department to capture diverse needs.
2. **Analysis:** Resolve conflicts (e.g., doctors want unlimited report access, but IT wants strict RBAC). Apply MoSCoW to prioritize.
3. **Specification:** Document the SRS following IEEE 29148.
4. **Validation:** Conduct Fagan inspections with stakeholders; verify each requirement is testable.
5. **Management:** Establish a Change Control Board (CCB) to handle requests like "Add telemedicine module."

**MoSCoW for HMS:**

| # | Requirement | Priority | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Patient registration & unique ID generation | **Must** | Without this, no other module works. |
| 2 | Electronic Medical Record (EMR) storage | **Must** | Core clinical data; legal requirement. |
| 3 | Doctor appointment scheduling | **Must** | Daily hospital operation. |
| 4 | Prescription generation & drug interaction check | **Must** | Patient safety. |
| 5 | Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) for staff | **Must** | Data security & compliance. |
| 6 | Integration with insurance APIs | **Should** | Important for billing but can be manual initially. |
| 7 | SMS/email appointment reminders | **Should** | Reduces no-shows. |
| 8 | Analytics dashboard for hospital KPIs | **Could** | Useful but not critical for day-1 launch. |
| 9 | AI-based diagnosis suggestions | **Won't (this release)** | High risk; defer to v2.0. |
| 10 | Multi-language UI (regional languages) | **Won't (this release)** | Future scope. |

**Count Check:**

$$
\vert M \vert + \vert S \vert + \vert C \vert + \vert W \vert = 5 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 10 \checkmark
$$

#### Part (b) — RTM Construction + Coverage Calculation (7 marks)

| Req ID | Description | Stakeholder Source | Design Module | Code Module | Test Case | Link Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **REQ-HMS-01** | Patient registration with Aadhaar-based ID | Reception Desk | `PatientMgmt` | `patient.c` | TC-001 | ✅ |
| **REQ-HMS-02** | EMR encrypted at rest (AES-256) | Medical Records | `DataSecurity` | `crypto.c` | TC-002 | ✅ |
| **REQ-HMS-03** | Doctor appointment booking (max 30/day) | Doctor | `Scheduler` | `schedule.c` | TC-003 | ✅ |
| **REQ-HMS-04** | Prescription with allergy alerts | Pharmacist | `Prescription` | `rx.c` | TC-004 | ✅ |
| **REQ-HMS-05** | RBAC with 4 roles (Admin, Doctor, Nurse, Receptionist) | IT Security | `AuthService` | `auth.c` | TC-005 | ✅ |
| **REQ-HMS-06** | Insurance claim generation (PDF) | Billing | `Billing` | `billing.c` | TC-006 | ✅ |

**Traceability Coverage Calculation:**

$$
T_{cov} = \frac{1}{R_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{6} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{4} r_{i,j} \right) \times \frac{1}{4} \times 100\%
$$

Since all six requirements are linked to all four artifact columns, the inner sum $= 4$ for each row:

$$
T_{cov} = \frac{1}{6} \times 6 \times \frac{1}{4} \times 4 \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Achieving **100%** traceability is the gold standard for safety-critical and regulated systems. KTU examiners award full marks when students *both* present the matrix *and* explicitly compute the coverage.

---

### Question B: Requirements Validation & Change Management `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Requirements Specification**. Explain the **IEEE 29148** standard template structure for a Software Requirements Specification (SRS). List and briefly explain the **eight quality attributes** of a good requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stakeholder submits a change request to *"add a mobile app to the existing web-based HMS."* Explain, with a suitable diagram, the **complete change management workflow** including impact analysis, Change Control Board (CCB) review, and re-validation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Requirements Specification + IEEE 29148 + Quality Attributes (7 marks)

**Definition:** Requirements Specification is the activity of producing a complete, precise, verifiable, and approved document (the SRS) that describes *what* the system shall do, *not how*.

**IEEE 29148:2018 SRS Template Structure:**

1. **Introduction** (Purpose, Scope, Definitions, References, Overview)
2. **Overall Description** (Product Perspective, User Characteristics, Constraints, Assumptions)
3. **Specific Requirements** (Functional, Performance, Interface, Design Constraints)
4. **Verification & Validation Criteria** (How each requirement will be tested)
5. **Appendices** (Data dictionaries, use cases, prototypes)

**Eight Quality Attributes (IEEE 830 / 29148):**

| # | Attribute | One-line Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Correct** | Accurately represents the stakeholder need. |
| 2 | **Unambiguous** | Has exactly one interpretation. |
| 3 | **Complete** | Covers all cases, including errors. |
| 4 | **Consistent** | Does not conflict with other requirements. |
| 5 | **Ranked/Prioritized** | Has an assigned importance (MoSCoW). |
| 6 | **Verifiable** | Can be objectively tested. |
| 7 | **Modifiable** | Easy to change while preserving history. |
| 8 | **Traceable** | Origin and destination clearly linked. |

#### Part (b) — Change Management Workflow (7 marks)

**Workflow Steps:**

1. **Change Request Submission** — Stakeholder submits *CR-2024-015: Add mobile app for HMS.*
2. **Logging & Classification** — Project Manager logs the CR and tags it as *Major — New Subsystem*.
3. **Impact Analysis** — Systems Engineer evaluates:
   - Affected REQs: REQ-HMS-01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06 (all currently web-based).
   - Estimated effort: **6 person-months**.
   - Risk: API rate limits, mobile security, OS fragmentation.
4. **CCB Review** — Change Control Board meets, evaluates cost-benefit, and either *Approves, Rejects, or Defers*.
5. **If Approved** — Update SRS, create new REQs (REQ-HMS-07 to REQ-HMS-12 for mobile), update design and test plans.
6. **Re-Validation** — Conduct Fagan inspection on the updated SRS; verify all new requirements are testable.
7. **Baseline & Communicate** — New SRS version released; all teams notified; RTM updated.

**Diagram of the Workflow:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stakeholder Submits Change Request] --> B[Project Manager Logs and Classifies]
    B --> C[Systems Engineer Conducts Impact Analysis]
    C --> D[CCB Review Meeting]
    D --> E{Decision}
    E -- Approved --> F[Update SRS with New REQs]
    F --> G[Re validate via Fagan Inspection]
    G --> H[Update RTM and Test Plans]
    H --> I[New Baseline Released]
    E -- Rejected --> J[Document Rationale and Notify Stakeholder]
    E -- Deferred --> K[Add to Future Release Backlog]
```

**Expected Length:** ~1.5 pages with the diagram.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the feedback loop** — Many students draw the RE process as a *linear* chain. This loses at least **1 mark**. Always show the closed loop from Validation back to Elicitation.
> 2. **Confusing "Need" with "Requirement"** — A need is a *user wish*; a requirement is a *measurable, testable specification*. Examiners deduct marks for interchangeable use.
> 3. **Missing MoSCoW justification** — Simply listing "M, S, C, W" without *justifying* each classification is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the RTM coverage calculation** — The RTM is worth **3 marks**; the calculation is worth **2 marks**. Students often skip the math.
> 5. **Forgetting IEEE 29148** — Always cite the *current* standard (post-2018). Citing the obsolete IEEE 830-1998 still gets partial credit but loses the "current best practice" mark.
> 6. **No industry linkage** — For a 14-mark question, mentioning the relevant standard (e.g., IEC 62304 for medical, ISO 26262 for automotive) demonstrates the *Engineering Practice* CO and adds 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Requirements Engineering = Elicit → Analyze → Specify → Validate → Manage (EA-SVM mnemonic).
- ✅ **Purpose:** Convert *implicit* stakeholder needs into *explicit, testable* specifications.
- ✅ **Why critical:** 70–80% of system defects originate here; 1:10:100 cost-of-change ratio across life cycle.
- ✅ **Five Elicitation Techniques (most-used in exams):** Interviews, Workshops, Observation, Surveys, Document Analysis, Prototyping.
- ✅ **MoSCoW:** Must, Should, Could, Won't. Always justify.
- ✅ **Eight Quality Attributes of a Good Requirement:** Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Prioritized, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable.
- ✅ **Hierarchy (4 levels):** Stakeholder Needs → StRS → SyRS → Subsystem Requirements. Each level refines the previous.
- ✅ **Types of Requirements:** Functional, Non-Functional (KURATES), Interface, Operational, Constraints.
- ✅ **Validation vs. Verification:** "Right system" vs. "System right." Validation = RE phase. Verification = design/test phase.
- ✅ **Standards to cite:** IEEE 29148:2018 (SRS template), ISO/IEC 25010 (NFR), INCOSE SE Handbook, IEEE 830-1998 (legacy).
- ✅ **Traceability:** RTM links each REQ to source, design, code, test. Target $T_{cov} = 100\%$ for safety-critical systems.
- ✅ **Change Management:** Log → Impact Analysis → CCB → Approve/Reject/Defer → Update SRS → Re-validate → Baseline.
- ✅ **Real-World Use Cases:** Aerospace (DO-178C), Medical (IEC 62304), Automotive (ISO 26262), IT (ITIL).
- ✅ **Cost-of-Change Multiplier:** $1 \times : 10 \times : 100 \times$ for Concept : Development : Operations.
- ✅ **SMART Test for any requirement:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- ✅ **Volatility Index:** $V = \frac{\Delta R}{R_{total}}$; aim for decreasing $V$ over iterations.
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Question:** "Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements with examples." Practice a 5-line answer with the KURATES mnemonic.
- ✅ **Always draw a diagram:** The closed-loop RE process or the 4-level hierarchy — visual answers score 1–2 extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Analysis and Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT865 - SYSTEMS ENGINEERING AND INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functional Analysis and Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Functional Analysis?

**Functional Analysis** is a systems engineering method used to systematically describe, decompose, and understand the **functions** that a system must perform to meet stakeholder needs. It transforms high-level, abstract mission statements (the "what") into a structured, verifiable set of operational, support, and interaction functions (the lower-level "whats"). It answers the question: **"What does the system have to *do* — without saying *how* it does it?"**

According to the **INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook** and the **SEBoK (Systems Engineering Body of Knowledge)**, Functional Analysis is the foundational translation step between *Stakeholder Needs* and a *Physical Solution* in the System Life Cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Functional Analysis and Allocation sits at the heart of the *Requirements Analysis* and *Functional Analysis* activities of the SE life cycle. It is a direct prerequisite to *Synthesis*, *Allocation*, and *Design Definition*.

## 1.2 What is Functional Allocation?

**Functional Allocation** is the logical process of mapping each defined function (the "what") onto a specific **physical element** of the system — that is, deciding *which subsystem, hardware item, software item, operator, or external system* will perform that function. It is the bridge from the *functional architecture* to the *physical architecture*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (INCOSE):** Functional allocation is the systems engineering activity of assigning the system functions identified during functional analysis to one or more elements of the system architecture, taking into account performance, reliability, cost, risk, and other constraints.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — Building a Restaurant

Imagine you want to open a restaurant. **Functional Analysis** is the act of listing everything the restaurant *must do*:

- Take orders
- Prepare food
- Serve food
- Bill the customer
- Clean the dining area

Notice that we are not yet saying *who* does what — only *what* must be done. The next step, **Functional Allocation**, is the act of deciding:

- A *chef* will *prepare food*
- A *waiter* will *take orders* and *serve food*
- A *cashier* (or POS system) will *bill the customer*
- A *janitor* will *clean the dining area*

The functions are the **verbs** (the actions). The people and equipment are the **nouns** (the physical resources). Functional analysis defines the verbs; functional allocation assigns them to the nouns. In a real KTU 2024 Scheme exam, examiners look for this clean separation.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Vocabulary

- **Top-level function** — the single sentence that describes the system's overall purpose.
- **Sub-function** — a child function in the functional decomposition tree.
- **Functional flow** — the sequence and parallelism of function execution.
- **Allocation matrix** — the traceability grid from function to physical element.
- **A measure of effectiveness (MoE)** — a top-level yardstick for success, expressed in units such as **km/h**, **dB**, **seconds**, or **$ cost/year**.
- **A performance requirement (KPP)** — a key performance parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Perform Functional Analysis?

A system is rarely a single black box. It is a hierarchy of interacting sub-systems, and its behaviour emerges from those interactions. Functional analysis provides three KTU-rewarded benefits:

1. **Traceability** — every lower-level design decision can be traced back to a *stakeholder need* through a chain of functions.
2. **Independence from implementation** — the same function set can be re-allocated to different physical solutions (e.g., human vs. machine), enabling trade studies.
3. **Validation & Verification (V&V) anchor** — once a function is defined, a corresponding test can be defined to verify it.

## 2.2 The Functional Decomposition Process (Stepwise)

The KTU 2024 Scheme module on System Engineering treats functional analysis as a recursive four-step procedure:

1. **Identify the top-level function** from the *Mission Statement* or *System Operational Concept (ConOps)*.
2. **Decompose** the top-level function into second-level sub-functions using the rule *"verb–noun"* (e.g., *convert energy*, *display data*, *transmit signal*).
3. **Refine** each sub-function to the next level until a sub-function can be implemented by a single *physical element* (the allocation threshold).
4. **Document** the result as a *Functional Hierarchy* (tree) and a *Functional Flow Block Diagram (FFBD)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Allocation Threshold (n+1 Rule):** Stop decomposing when each leaf function can be implemented by **one identifiable physical element** (hardware, software, operator, facility, or external system). Going deeper is over-engineering; stopping higher leaves "orphan" functions with no clear owner.

## 2.3 Functional Allocation — Decision Criteria

When a function is ready to be allocated, the engineer selects a physical element using a weighted decision matrix. The standard KTU-recommended criteria, in order of weight, are:

1. **Functional feasibility** — Can this element physically perform the function?
2. **Performance margin** — Does it meet the required MoE with margin?
3. **Reliability & availability** — MTBF, MTTR, availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
4. **Cost (LCC)** — Life-Cycle Cost including acquisition, operation, and disposal.
5. **Risk** — Technology Readiness Level (TRL), integration risk.
6. **Human factors** — Can a human safely and effectively do it? (sometimes called the "Man or Machine" question).

## 2.4 The Man-Machine (Human vs. Automatic) Allocation Heuristics

Classic KTU/INCOSE heuristics for assigning a function to a *human* versus a *machine*:

| Criterion | Human Preferred When | Machine Preferred When |
|---|---|---|
| Decision complexity | High, novel situations | Repetitive, rule-based |
| Speed of response | Moderate | Sub-second |
| Pattern recognition | Required | Not required |
| Hazard exposure | Low | High (e.g., nuclear, deep-sea) |
| Endurance | Short task | Long, monotonous task |
| Power output | Low–moderate | High sustained |

## 2.5 KTU Formula & Symbol Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value, and `\mid` for "such that / divides", to keep the markdown tables safe.

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | System availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| $LCC$ | Life-Cycle Cost | $ or ₹ |
| $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability formula | unitless |
| $f_{total}$ | Total number of leaf functions | integer |
| $n_{PE}$ | Number of physical elements | integer |
| $R = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Series reliability (independent elements) | unitless |
| $R = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | Parallel (redundant) reliability | unitless |
| $TRL$ | Technology Readiness Level | 1–9 scale |
| $\text{coverage} = \dfrac{n_{allocated}}{f_{total}}$ | Allocation coverage metric | unitless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Functional analysis and allocation are used in:

- **Aerospace** — NASA and ISRO allocate every spacecraft function to a subsystem (EPS, C&DH, TTC, Payload).
- **Automotive** — *ADAS* features are allocated to ECUs, sensors, and actuators.
- **Medical devices** — IEC 62304 requires documented software function allocation.
- **Defence** — Indian MoD's DRDO follows a formal functional allocation for missile, radar, and UAV programmes.
- **Civil Engineering (KTU 2024 cross-link)** — building services (HVAC, lighting, fire safety) are allocated to mechanical, electrical, and plumbing sub-systems using the same logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

In this section, we will (a) derive the **availability formula** used in allocation decisions, (b) derive the **parallel redundancy reliability** expression, and (c) execute a complete **Functional Analysis & Allocation walkthrough** for a small unmanned aerial system — exactly the kind of question a KTU 2024 scheme examiner loves to set for 14 marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Availability Formula $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$

Availability is the *fraction of time a system is operationally ready*. A system alternates between two states: **up** (operational) and **down** (failed and being repaired). Let the average time spent in each state be:

- Mean Time Between Failures $= MTBF$ (hours of operation between two failures)
- Mean Time To Repair $= MTTR$ (hours of repair after a failure)

The total cycle time is the sum of the two states:

$$
T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

Availability is the ratio of "useful" time to total time:

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Cycle Time}} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

To see the limiting behaviour:

- If $MTTR \to 0$ (instant repair), then $A \to 1$ — perfect availability.
- If $MTBF \to 0$ (constant failure), then $A \to 0$ — system is never available.

The KTU-relevant unitless availability is often multiplied by 100 to give a percentage $A_{\%} = 100 \cdot A$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Parallel (Redundant) Reliability Expression

Consider $n$ independent physical elements, each with reliability $R_i$, that are wired in **parallel** — that is, the system fails only if *all* of them fail. The probability that element $i$ fails is $(1 - R_i)$. The probability that *all* $n$ elements fail simultaneously is, by the independence assumption:

$$
P_{all\ fail} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)
$$

The system survives (is reliable) if it is **not** the case that all elements failed:

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)
$$

For the special case of two **identical** parallel elements with reliability $R$ each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{parallel,2} &= 1 - (1-R)(1-R) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 2R + R^{2}) \\
&= 2R - R^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the equation that justifies the "dual-redundant flight computer" or "dual-redundant battery bus" in a KTU exam answer on safety-critical allocation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Functional Analysis & Allocation of a Small Survey Drone

This worked example is the type of 14-mark sub-question (a) that a KTU 2024 ESE paper sets. Follow every step.

### Step 1 — Top-Level Function (from the ConOps)

> *"The drone shall conduct an autonomous aerial survey of a 2 km × 2 km agricultural field and return geo-tagged multispectral images to a ground station."*

The **top-level function** is therefore:

$$
F_0 = \text{Perform autonomous aerial survey}
$$

### Step 2 — First-Level Functional Decomposition

Break $F_0$ into seven sub-functions $F_{1.1}$ to $F_{1.7}$:

- $F_{1.1}$: Acquire position
- $F_{1.2}$: Acquire attitude
- $F_{1.3}$: Capture multispectral image
- $F_{1.4}$: Store image
- $F_{1.5}$: Transmit image
- $F_{1.6}$: Provide lift
- $F_{1.7}$: Manage onboard power

### Step 3 — Continue Decomposition to Allocation Threshold

For illustration, decompose $F_{1.5}$ further:

- $F_{1.5.1}$: Modulate signal
- $F_{1.5.2}$: Up-convert to RF
- $F_{1.5.3}$: Amplify RF
- $F_{1.5.4}$: Radiate via antenna

At this point each leaf can be implemented by one physical element — the *allocation threshold* is reached.

### Step 4 — Build the Functional Allocation Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Leaf function } F_L \;\; &\longmapsto \;\; \text{Physical element } P_j \\
F_{1.1} &\longmapsto \text{GPS Receiver (HW)} \\
F_{1.2} &\longmapsto \text{IMU + Magnetometer (HW)} \\
F_{1.3} &\longmapsto \text{Multispectral Camera (HW)} \\
F_{1.4} &\longmapsto \text{Onboard SD card (HW)} \\
F_{1.5.1} &\longmapsto \text{Modem firmware (SW)} \\
F_{1.5.2} &\longmapsto \text{RF up-converter (HW)} \\
F_{1.5.3} &\longmapsto \text{PA — Power Amplifier (HW)} \\
F_{1.5.4} &\longmapsto \text{Antenna (HW)} \\
F_{1.6} &\longmapsto \text{BLDC motor + propeller (HW)} \\
F_{1.7} &\longmapsto \text{Li-Po battery (HW + BMS SW)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate the Allocation Coverage Metric

$$
\text{coverage} = \dfrac{n_{allocated}}{f_{total}} = \dfrac{10}{10} = 1.00 = 100\%
$$

A coverage of $1.00$ means **every leaf function is owned by a physical element** — a healthy result.

### Step 6 — Quick Availability Check on a Redundant Pair

Suppose the IMU has $R_{IMU} = 0.98$ and a backup IMU of equal reliability is wired in parallel. Using the derived parallel formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{IMU,parallel} &= 2R - R^{2} \\
&= 2(0.98) - (0.98)^{2} \\
&= 1.96 - 0.9604 \\
&= 0.9996
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme examiner will give full marks only if the *steps*, *units*, and *final numeric* are shown exactly like this. A bare final answer of 0.9996 with no working loses 4 to 5 marks.

### Step 7 — Document the Functional Flow (FFBD)

The Functional Flow Block Diagram for $F_0$ in pseudo-notation is:

$$
F_0 \rightarrow (F_{1.6} \parallel F_{1.7}) \rightarrow F_{1.1} \rightarrow F_{1.2} \rightarrow F_{1.3} \rightarrow (F_{1.4} \leftrightarrow F_{1.5})
$$

The notation $\parallel$ means *concurrent execution*; $\leftrightarrow$ means *bidirectional data flow* between the storage and the transmitter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following four Mermaid diagrams map the lifecycle of a functional analysis & allocation exercise, the functional hierarchy, the allocation matrix, and the FFBD for the survey drone example from §3.3. All node IDs are alphanumeric with a letter prefix, and labels contain no markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 Functional Analysis & Allocation — Top-Level Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stakeholder Needs] --> A2[ConOps and Mission Statement]
    A2 --> A3[Define Top Level Function F0]
    A3 --> A4[Functional Decomposition]
    A4 --> A5{Allocation Threshold Reached?}
    A5 -- No --> A4
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Functional Architecture FFBD]
    A6 --> A7[Identify Physical Elements]
    A7 --> A8[Functional Allocation Matrix]
    A8 --> A9[Trade Study and Verification]
    A9 --> A10[Physical Architecture Baseline]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Hierarchy for the Survey Drone

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[F0 Perform Autonomous Aerial Survey]
    B0 --> B11[F1.1 Acquire Position]
    B0 --> B12[F1.2 Acquire Attitude]
    B0 --> B13[F1.3 Capture Multispectral Image]
    B0 --> B14[F1.4 Store Image]
    B0 --> B15[F1.5 Transmit Image]
    B0 --> B16[F1.6 Provide Lift]
    B0 --> B17[F1.7 Manage Onboard Power]
    B15 --> B151[F1.5.1 Modulate Signal]
    B15 --> B152[F1.5.2 Up Convert to RF]
    B15 --> B153[F1.5.3 Amplify RF]
    B15 --> B154[F1.5.4 Radiate via Antenna]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional-to-Physical Allocation Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C11[F1.1 Acquire Position] --> C11P[GPS Receiver HW]
    C12[F1.2 Acquire Attitude] --> C12P[IMU plus Magnetometer HW]
    C13[F1.3 Capture Image] --> C13P[Multispectral Camera HW]
    C14[F1.4 Store Image] --> C14P[SD Card Storage HW]
    C15A[F1.5.1 Modulate Signal] --> C15AP[Modem Firmware SW]
    C15B[F1.5.2 Up Convert to RF] --> C15BP[RF Up Converter HW]
    C15C[F1.5.3 Amplify RF] --> C15CP[Power Amplifier HW]
    C15D[F1.5.4 Radiate] --> C15DP[Antenna HW]
    C16[F1.6 Provide Lift] --> C16P[BLDC Motor plus Propeller HW]
    C17[F1.7 Manage Power] --> C17P[Li Po Battery plus BMS HW and SW]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Flow Block Diagram (FFBD) for the Survey Drone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[F0 Aerial Survey] --> D6[F1.6 Provide Lift]
    D0 --> D7[F1.7 Manage Power]
    D6 --> D1[F1.1 Acquire Position]
    D7 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[F1.2 Acquire Attitude]
    D2 --> D3[F1.3 Capture Image]
    D3 --> D4[F1.4 Store Image]
    D4 <--> D5[F1.5 Transmit Image]
    D5 --> D8[Ground Station Receives Data]
```

---

## 4.5 The "n+1" Allocation Threshold Rule — Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Decompose Function Fn] --> E2{Can ONE Physical Element Implement Fn?}
    E2 -- Yes --> E3[STOP Decomposition and Allocate to Element Pj]
    E2 -- No --> E4[Decompose Fn to Fn plus 1]
    E4 --> E1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These Mermaid block diagrams are the KTU-accepted substitute for hand-drawn FFBDs. Examiners award full marks for **clear, complete, and traceable** diagrams — not for artistic shading.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The questions below are calibrated to the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice. Each sub-question carries a uniform 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Functional Analysis. Why is it performed before allocating functions to physical elements? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Functional Analysis is the systematic process of identifying, describing, and decomposing the *functions* a system must perform to satisfy stakeholder needs, expressed in a *verb–noun* form and independent of any physical implementation. It is performed before allocation because it (i) decouples *what* the system must do from *how* it does it, (ii) provides a traceable baseline for verification, and (iii) enables multiple physical solutions to be trade-studied against a single functional set. (3 marks)

### Q2. Distinguish between Functional Analysis and Functional Allocation with one example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Functional Analysis identifies *what* the system must do; Functional Allocation decides *who/what physical element* will do it. Example: Functional Analysis says *the system shall detect obstacle*; Functional Allocation assigns it to *ultrasonic sensor (HW) + interrupt handler (SW)*. The analysis step produces the function list; the allocation step produces the physical-element map. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Any 14-mark answer in Module 1 is expected to be roughly **3 handwritten pages**, contain a clear **diagram**, and end with a **concluding statement** that links the answer back to the *system life cycle*. Missing any one of these loses 1–2 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a suitable example, explain the step-by-step procedure of Functional Analysis. `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Identify the top-level function** $F_0$ from the mission statement. For a *home security alarm system*, $F_0$ = *Detect and report unauthorised intrusion*. (1 mark)
2. **First-level decomposition** of $F_0$ into $F_{1.1}$ *Sense motion*, $F_{1.2}$ *Sense door/window open*, $F_{1.3}$ *Process sensor data*, $F_{1.4}$ *Raise local alarm*, $F_{1.5}$ *Notify owner*, $F_{1.6}$ *Log event*, $F_{1.7}$ *Power the system*. (2 marks)
3. **Refine** each first-level sub-function to a *verb–noun* form. For example, $F_{1.3}$ further breaks into $F_{1.3.1}$ *Acquire ADC sample*, $F_{1.3.2}$ *Apply threshold rule*, $F_{1.3.3}$ *Trigger notification*. (1 mark)
4. **Stop at the allocation threshold (n+1 rule)**: when each leaf function can be implemented by a single physical element. (1 mark)
5. **Document** the result in two forms: a *Functional Hierarchy* (tree) and a *Functional Flow Block Diagram (FFBD)* showing sequence and parallelism. (1 mark)
6. **Validate** by ensuring every leaf traces back to a stakeholder need. (1 mark)

#### (b) Describe the criteria and methods used in Functional Allocation. Illustrate with the man-versus-machine decision. `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

The six standard KTU/INCOSE allocation criteria are: (i) functional feasibility, (ii) performance margin, (iii) reliability/availability, (iv) life-cycle cost, (v) risk/TRL, and (vi) human factors. They are combined in a **weighted decision matrix**: each function is scored against each criterion, the score is multiplied by the criterion's weight, and the physical element with the highest total weighted score is selected. (3 marks)

A common and KTU-favourite sub-problem is the **Man-versus-Machine** allocation. The classical heuristics are:

- Allocate to a **human** when the task requires judgement, novel decision-making, or low-frequency, low-repetitive work (e.g., *legal arbitration*).
- Allocate to a **machine** when the task is repetitive, high-speed, hazardous, or sustained (e.g., *welding on a moving assembly line*).
- **Co-allocation** is used for safety-critical functions (e.g., *aircraft autopilot + pilot override*) where both human and machine share the function with arbitration logic. (2 marks)

The decision is *revisable*: as TRL rises, functions that were once allocated to a human may migrate to a machine. (1 mark) Concluding sentence linking back to the SE life cycle: *Functional Allocation is the gate through which functional design becomes physical design*. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the concept of Functional Decomposition and the **Allocation Threshold (n+1) Rule**. `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Functional Decomposition** is the recursive division of a top-level function into smaller, fully traceable sub-functions, each expressed in a *verb–noun* form. The aim is to make the function set complete, non-overlapping, and testable. (2 marks)

The **n+1 Rule (Allocation Threshold)** states that decomposition stops at the level ($n+1$) where each leaf function can be implemented by *one* identifiable physical element — be it hardware, software, a human operator, a facility, or an external system. (2 marks)

**Why the rule matters:** Decomposition that is *too shallow* leaves "orphan" functions with no clear owner, leading to integration surprises. Decomposition that is *too deep* over-engineers the documentation, obscures traceability, and constrains the trade space unnecessarily. The rule therefore provides a *pragmatic stopping criterion*. (2 marks)

A one-line rule of thumb for the KTU answer: *Decompose until you can say "this one function is owned by this one element, and the test for that element proves the function"*. (1 mark)

#### (b) Construct a Functional Allocation Matrix for a **Smart Irrigation System** and compute the allocation coverage. `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Top-level function:** $F_0$ = *Irrigate field based on soil moisture and weather forecast*.

**Decomposition (first level):**

- $F_{1.1}$: Sense soil moisture
- $F_{1.2}$: Sense ambient temperature and humidity
- $F_{1.3}$: Fetch weather forecast
- $F_{1.4}$: Compute irrigation need
- $F_{1.5}$: Open/close water valve
- $F_{1.6}$: Display status
- $F_{1.7}$: Power the controller

**Allocation Matrix** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{1.1} &\longmapsto \text{Capacitive soil-moisture sensor (HW)} \\
F_{1.2} &\longmapsto \text{DHT22 sensor (HW)} \\
F_{1.3} &\longmapsto \text{Wi-Fi module + weather API (HW + SW)} \\
F_{1.4} &\longmapsto \text{Microcontroller firmware (SW)} \\
F_{1.5} &\longmapsto \text{Solenoid valve + MOSFET driver (HW)} \\
F_{1.6} &\longmapsto \text{LCD display (HW)} \\
F_{1.7} &\longmapsto \text{Solar panel + battery + charge controller (HW + SW)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Allocation Coverage:**

$$
\text{coverage} = \dfrac{n_{allocated}}{f_{total}} = \dfrac{7}{7} = 1.00
$$

(2 marks) A coverage of $1.00$ confirms that every leaf function is owned. Concluding remark (2 marks): the matrix is the single document that bridges the *functional architecture* and the *physical architecture*, and is the input to the next SE life-cycle activity — *Synthesis and Design Definition*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing "what" and "how" in the function definition.** A function *must* be implementation-free. Writing "sense with a thermistor" instead of "sense temperature" is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Skipping the n+1 stopping rule.** Examiners will not credit a tree that decomposes only one or two levels — they expect *at least* the allocation-threshold explanation.
> 3. **No diagram.** A 14-mark question without a functional hierarchy, FFBD, or allocation-matrix diagram attracts an automatic 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **No traceability statement.** Closing without saying "every leaf traces back to a stakeholder need" loses the last synthesis mark.
> 5. **No units or numbers.** If the question asks for a metric, write the formula *and* the numeric value *and* the unit (e.g., $A = 0.999$, dimensionless, not just "0.999").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Functional Analysis** = the *what*. **Functional Allocation** = the *who/how-architecture*.
- Every function should be in **verb–noun** form and implementation-free.
- The **Allocation Threshold (n+1 Rule)** stops decomposition when each leaf is implementable by *one* physical element.
- The **Functional Hierarchy** (tree) and the **FFBD** are the two required documentation artefacts.
- The **Allocation Matrix** is the traceability grid from function to physical element.
- The **allocation coverage** metric must equal $1.00$ at the end of a complete analysis.
- The **availability formula** is $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ (unitless).
- The **parallel-redundancy reliability** is $R = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$.
- **Man–machine allocation** uses a weighted decision matrix plus classical heuristics: humans for judgement, machines for repetition/hazard/speed.
- **TRL** rises during the life cycle; allocations are revisable.
- Always close a 14-mark answer with a **life-cycle link**: *"This functional allocation feeds directly into the Synthesis and Design Definition phase of the SE Vee model."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Architecture and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT865 - SYSTEMS ENGINEERING AND INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Architecture and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **System Architecture** is the *conceptual model* that defines the **structure, behavior, and integration** of a system. It describes the system's components, their relationships to each other and to the environment, and the principles governing its design and evolution.

According to the **INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook** and KTU's 2024 PECPT865 syllabus, system architecture establishes the foundational framework upon which all subsequent design, integration, and verification activities are built. It is a **bridge between stakeholder needs** (captured in stakeholder requirements) and the **detailed design and implementation** phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Module 1):**  
> *"System Architecture is the arrangement of components, their interactions, and the governing principles that guide system design, providing a structured representation of the system's form and function across its lifecycle."*

### Sub-Definitions Aligned to KTU Module Outcomes:

| Term | Definition | KTU CO Mapping |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **Form (Physical Architecture)** | The physical allocation of functions to hardware, software, and human operators | CO1, CO2 |
| **Function (Functional Architecture)** | The independent functions, their sub-functions, and the data/control flows between them | CO1, CO2 |
| **Behavior** | The state-dependent response of the system to internal and external stimuli | CO1 |
| **Trade-off** | A documented decision process comparing alternative design solutions against criteria | CO3 |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a house construction project.** Before laying a single brick, an architect draws **blueprints** showing where each room sits, how water pipes connect, where electrical wiring routes, and how load-bearing walls support the roof.

> **The blueprint IS the architecture.** It doesn't tell you the color of the paint or the brand of the door handle (that's *detailed design*), but it captures **every major decision** about how the house is structured.

In the same way, **System Architecture** for a satellite, a smart grid, or a healthcare information system captures:
- **What are the major subsystems?** (Payload, Bus, Ground Station ↔ Kitchen, Plumbing, Electrical)
- **How do they talk to each other?** (Telemetry links, command channels ↔ Water pipes, wires)
- **What are the boundaries?** (What stays inside vs. what interacts with the environment)
- **What principles govern future changes?** (Modularity, open standards, scalability)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Intuition:**  
> *Architecture = The "What and Where" of the system.  
> Detailed Design = The "How exactly."*

---

## 1.3 Why Architecture Matters in Engineering (KTU 2024 Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pareto Principle in Systems Engineering:**  
> **80% of a system's lifecycle cost** is committed during the **first 20%** of the design phase (the architectural stage). Getting the architecture wrong locks in cost overruns, schedule slips, and integration failures that are **virtually impossible to fix later**.

This is why the KTU 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis (typically **12-14 marks in ESE** per module) on architectural concepts — it is the **single highest-leverage activity** in the entire systems engineering lifecycle.

### Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:

- **Modularity Index ($M$)**: A measure of how decomposable a system is, computed as the ratio of intra-module cohesion to inter-module coupling.
- **Interface Density ($I_d$)**: Number of interfaces per functional block, ideally kept below **3-5** for high-reliability systems.
- **Architectural Stability**: The percentage of architectural decisions that remain unchanged after the **Preliminary Design Review (PDR)**. Industry benchmark: **≥ 85%**.
- **Time-to-Integration**: Days from component availability to first successful system-level test. KTU benchmark for modular systems: **< 30 days**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of System Architecture (Core KTU Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Module 1 explicitly identifies **three architectural pillars** that every student must internalize:

### Pillar 1: **Functional Architecture**
- Describes **what the system does**, independent of *how* it is implemented.
- Built from the **functional decomposition** of stakeholder requirements.
- Delivered as: **Functional Flow Block Diagrams (FFBD)**, **N² Charts**, and **Functional Trees**.

### Pillar 2: **Physical Architecture**
- Describes **how the functions are physically realized** — by hardware, software, personnel, procedures, or facilities.
- Answers: "Which subsystem performs this function? Where does it live? What are its mass, volume, power, and cost budgets?"
- Delivered as: **Physical Block Diagrams**, **Bill of Materials (BoM)**, and **3D CAD models**.

### Pillar 3: **Behavioral / Dynamic Architecture**
- Describes **how the system responds over time** to inputs, modes, and states.
- Captures: Mode transitions (e.g., Launch → Orbit → De-orbit), state machines, timing constraints, and **System of Systems (SoS)** emergent behavior.
- Delivered as: **State Transition Diagrams**, **Sequence Diagrams**, and **Timing/Schedule Sheets**.

---

## 1.5 Visual Summary — The Architectural Hierarchy

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                    │   STAKEHOLDER NEEDS / MoEs   │   ← Mission Level
                    └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                                   │
                    ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐
                    │  SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE (3 Pillars) │
                    │  ┌────────┬────────┬────────┐  │
                    │  │Functional│Physical│Behavioral│  │   ← System Level
                    │  └────────┴────────┴────────┘  │
                    └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                                   │
                    ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐
                    │   DETAILED DESIGN & I&T      │   ← Subsystem Level
                    └──────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hint:** When asked "Define System Architecture," always include the **three pillars** (Functional, Physical, Behavioral). Examiners in KTU 2024 ESE reward students who mention *all three* with full 3 marks.

---

## 1.6 Position Within the Systems Engineering V-Model

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly anchors Module 1 within the **V-Model** of systems engineering. System Architecture and Design sits at the **apex (left side) of the V**, immediately after Stakeholder Requirements Definition and before Subsystem Design.

| V-Model Phase | Deliverable | KTU Module Reference |
|---------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Stakeholder Needs | Mission Need Statement | Module 1 (intro) |
| **System Architecture** | **Functional/Physical/Behavioral Arch.** | **Module 1 (Core)** |
| Subsystem Design | Subsystem Specs | Module 2 |
| Detailed Design | Component Specs | Module 2 |
| Implementation | Hardware/Software Build | Module 3 |
| Integration & Test | System Validation | Module 3 |

This positioning is **non-negotiable** in any KTU ESE answer: always state *which phase* of the V-Model you are discussing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architectural Design Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 Module 1 specifies a **five-step architectural design process** that follows the ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 standard. Below is the exhaustive, structured breakdown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Sequence (Must appear in exam answers in this order):**
> 1. Stakeholder Need Analysis → 2. Functional Decomposition → 3. Physical Allocation → 4. Trade Study → 5. Baseline Architecture Selection

### Step 1: Stakeholder Need Analysis
- **Why:** Without a clear understanding of *who* the system serves and *what problem* it solves, architecture becomes guesswork.
- **How:** Capture needs via interviews, surveys, and operational concept documents (OpsCon/ConOps). Translate needs into **measurable requirements** using SMART criteria (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
- **Output:** Stakeholder Requirements Specification (StRS).

### Step 2: Functional Decomposition
- **Why:** Complex systems cannot be designed as monoliths. Decomposition creates a hierarchy of independent, testable functions.
- **How:** Apply the **"divide and conquer"** principle recursively:
  - Top-level: 1 function ("Provide Service X")
  - Level 1: 3-7 major functions
  - Level 2: 5-10 sub-functions per major function
  - Stop when each leaf function is implementable by a single component.
- **Output:** Functional Flow Block Diagram (FFBD), Functional Tree, N² Chart.

### Step 3: Physical Allocation
- **Why:** Functions must be *assigned* to physical realizers (hardware, software, humans, procedures).
- **How:** Use **Design Allocation Matrices** mapping functions to physical entities. Apply the **"right tool for the job"** principle — compute-intensive functions → software; high-power functions → hardware; judgment-intensive functions → humans.
- **Output:** Physical Block Diagram, Preliminary BOM, Interface Control Documents (ICDs).

### Step 4: Trade Study
- **Why:** Multiple valid allocations exist. The architect must select the *best* one based on weighted criteria.
- **How:** Execute the **Weighted Trade Study Algorithm** (detailed in Section 2.4).
- **Output:** Trade Study Report documenting alternatives, scoring, sensitivity, and recommendation.

### Step 5: Baseline Architecture Selection & Review
- **Why:** A formal baseline freezes the architecture for downstream design.
- **How:** Conduct a **System Requirements Review (SRR)** and a **Preliminary Design Review (PDR)**. Capture changes via a **Configuration Control Board (CCB)**.
- **Output:** Baselines (Functional, Allocated, Product), reviewed by stakeholders.

---

## 2.2 Core Architectural Concepts (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 Functional Decomposition Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 mandates the following decomposition rules:**

| Rule | Statement | Rationale |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| **R1: Mutually Exclusive** | No sub-function may overlap with another sub-function at the same level | Prevents double-counting |
| **R2: Collectively Exhaustive** | All sub-functions must, when combined, perform the parent function | Prevents "missing" capability |
| **R3: 3-7 Sub-functions** | Each function decomposes into **3-7** sub-functions (Miller's Law) | Cognitive load limit |
| **R4: Single Realization Path** | Each leaf function maps to exactly one physical component | Traceability |

### 2.2.2 Architecture Frameworks (Standard Vocabulary)

| Framework | Origin | Primary Use | KTU 2024 Status |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **DoDAF (DoD Architecture Framework)** | U.S. DoD | Military systems | **Must know** (8 views: AV, OV, SV, DIV, etc.) |
| **Zachman Framework** | John Zachman (1987) | Enterprise architecture | Recommended reading |
| **TOGAF (The Open Group Architecture Framework)** | The Open Group | Enterprise IT | Industry standard |
| **UML/SysML** | OMG | Software-intensive systems | **Must know** (Block, IBD, StM, Sequence) |
| **ArchiMate** | The Open Group | Enterprise modeling | Optional |

### 2.2.3 Architectural Views (DoDAF — KTU Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 frequently asks:** "List the architectural views in DoDAF." The standard answer is the **8-view set**:

1. **All Viewpoint (AV-1, AV-2)** — Project metadata, glossary
2. **Capability Viewpoint (CV-1, CV-2, CV-3, CV-4, CV-5, CV-6, CV-7)** — Strategic capabilities
3. **Data and Information Viewpoint (DIV-1, DIV-2, DIV-3)** — Data structures
4. **Operations Viewpoint (OV-1, OV-2, OV-3, OV-4, OV-5, OV-6a, OV-6b, OV-6c)** — Operational nodes, activities, events
5. **Project Viewpoint (PV-1, PV-2, PV-3)** — Programs and timelines
6. **Services Viewpoint (SvcV-1, SvcV-2, SvcV-3a, SvcV-3b, SvcV-4, SvcV-5, SvcV-6, SvcV-7, SvcV-8, SvcV-9, SvcV-10a, SvcV-10b, SvcV-10c)** — Services and interfaces
7. **Standards Viewpoint (StdV-1, StdV-2)** — Applicable standards
8. **Systems Viewpoint (SV-1, SV-2, SV-3, SV-4, SV-5, SV-6, SV-7, SV-8, SV-9, SV-10a, SV-10b, SV-10c)** — System composition, interfaces, states

---

## 2.3 The Three Pillars — Deep Technical Detail

### Pillar 1: Functional Architecture

**Definition (KTU 2024):** A domain-independent description of what the system must do, expressed as a hierarchy of functions, their interrelationships, and the data/control flows between them.

**Key Diagrams:**
- **FFBD (Functional Flow Block Diagram):** Shows top-down decomposition.
- **N² Chart (N-Squared Diagram):** Shows all pairwise interfaces between $N$ functions in a single matrix. Critical for interface analysis.
- **Functional Tree:** Strict hierarchical decomposition (no cross-links).

**N² Chart Notation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N \times N \text{ matrix where entry } (i,j) \text{ represents the interface} \\
\text{from function } i \text{ (row) to function } j \text{ (column)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Utility:** The N² chart is the **only** tool that reveals *all* interfaces, including those hidden by hierarchical decomposition. KTU examiners love asking: "How many interfaces does a 5-function system have?" (Answer: $5^2 = 25$ entries, of which the diagonal represents the function itself, so $25 - 5 = 20$ external interfaces.)

### Pillar 2: Physical Architecture

**Definition:** The mapping of functions onto physical realizers (hardware/software/human/procedural) and the physical arrangement of these realizers.

**Key Metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass Budget Utilization} &= \frac{M_{\text{used}}}{M_{\text{allocated}}} \times 100\% \\
\text{Power Budget Utilization} &= \frac{P_{\text{used}}}{P_{\text{allocated}}} \times 100\% \\
\text{Volume Utilization} &= \frac{V_{\text{used}}}{V_{\text{allocated}}} \times 100\% \\
\text{Cost Performance Index (CPI)} &= \frac{\text{Earned Value (EV)}}{\text{Actual Cost (AC)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Utility:** Physical architecture translates the *logical* functional design into *realizable* engineering terms. It is the input to **CAD layout**, **manufacturing planning**, and **logistics modeling**.

### Pillar 3: Behavioral / Dynamic Architecture

**Definition:** Describes the system's *temporal* behavior — modes, states, transitions, and timing.

**Key Diagrams:**
- **State Transition Diagram (StM):** States as nodes, transitions as directed edges with trigger/condition/action labels.
- **Sequence Diagram (UML/SysML):** Time-ordered message exchange between components.
- **Timing Diagram:** Waveform-style representation of signal/value evolution.

**Modes of Operation (Standard KTU Set for Complex Systems):**

| Mode | Purpose | Example (Satellite) |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| **Startup** | Initialize and verify | Post-launch power-up |
| **Nominal/Operational** | Primary mission | Imaging, downlink |
| **Standby** | Low-power idle | Eclipse period |
| **Degraded** | Reduced capability | Single-string failure |
| **Emergency/Safe** | Protect assets | Anomaly response |
| **Shutdown/Disposal** | End-of-life | De-orbit burn |

---

## 2.4 The Weighted Trade Study Algorithm (High-Yield Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most tested formula** in KTU 2024 Module 1 examinations. Memorize it.

**Step 1:** Identify $k$ candidate alternatives and $n$ evaluation criteria.

**Step 2:** Assign weights $w_i$ to each criterion such that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1.0 \quad \text{(or 100\%)}
$$

**Step 3:** Score each alternative $A_j$ on each criterion $C_i$ on a scale (typically 1-10):

$$
s_{ji} \in [1, 10]
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **Weighted Score** for each alternative:

$$
S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ji}
$$

**Step 5:** The recommended alternative is:

$$
A^* = \arg\max_{j} \left\{ S_j \right\}
$$

**Step 6: Sensitivity Analysis:** Vary each $w_i$ by $\pm 10\%$ and verify that $A^*$ remains optimal. If it changes, the decision is **sensitive** and requires further study.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trap:** Students often forget the **sensitivity analysis step** (Step 6). KTU examiners allocate 2-3 marks specifically for this. Always include it in your answer.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table before any KTU exam on Module 1.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Range | KTU Use |
|---|----------------|-------------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ji}$ | Weighted Trade Study Score | Dimensionless [0, 10] | **Highest yield** |
| 2 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ | Weight normalization constraint | Sum = 1.0 or 100% | Always |
| 3 | $A^* = \arg\max_j S_j$ | Optimal alternative selection | Index $j$ | Algorithm step 5 |
| 4 | $I_{ext} = N^2 - N$ | External interfaces in N² chart | Count | Interface analysis |
| 5 | $\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}$ | Cost Performance Index | Ratio, ideal = 1.0 | Earned Value Mgmt |
| 6 | $\text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}$ | Schedule Performance Index | Ratio, ideal = 1.0 | Schedule tracking |
| 7 | $M = \frac{\text{Intra-module Cohesion}}{\text{Inter-module Coupling}}$ | Modularity Index (qualitative) | Higher is better | Design quality |
| 8 | $T_{RL} = 1 \text{ to } 9$ | Technology Readiness Level | 1=basic, 9=proven | Risk assessment |
| 9 | $N_{func} = \prod_{i=1}^{L} k_i$ | Total leaf functions (if $k_i$ branches/level) | Count | Decomposition scale |
| 10 | $A_{stable} \geq 85\%$ | Architectural stability post-PDR | Percentage | Maturity gate |

> **Critical Note on Table:** All vertical bars for absolute values or conditional statements are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $s_{ji} \in \vert 1, 10 \vert$ would be written as $s_{ji} \in [1, 10]$) to maintain Markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Architectural Patterns (KTU-Mandated)

| Pattern | Description | When to Use | Example |
|---------|-------------|-------------|---------|
| **Layered (Tiered)** | Strict hierarchy: presentation → logic → data | Information systems | Web apps, OS kernels |
| **Client-Server** | Service providers and requesters | Distributed systems | Email, banking |
| **Pipe-and-Filter** | Stream of data through sequential filters | Signal/data processing | Audio pipeline |
| **Publish-Subscribe** | Decoupled producers/consumers via broker | IoT, real-time | MQTT brokers |
| **Microservices** | Independent, loosely-coupled services | Cloud-native | Netflix, Amazon |
| **Modular Monolith** | Single deployable, internal modules | Early-stage products | Startups |
| **Event-Driven** | Asynchronous event handling | Reactive systems | Trading platforms |
| **System of Systems (SoS)** | Constituent systems retain independent operation | Defense, smart cities | Federated satellite comms |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> System Architecture and Design is fundamentally a **conceptual and algorithmic** discipline rather than a calculus-heavy one. The "derivations" in this section focus on **algorithmic procedures**, **scoring logic**, and **decomposition mathematics** that the KTU 2024 scheme rewards in ESE answers.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: External Interface Count in an N² Chart

> **Problem Statement:** Given a system decomposed into $N$ top-level functions, derive the total number of external interfaces that must be defined and managed in the Interface Control Document (ICD).

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:

**Step 1 — Define the N² Matrix Structure:**

A complete N² chart is a square matrix with $N$ rows and $N$ columns, where each cell $(i, j)$ represents the interface from function $i$ to function $j$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total cells in N}^2 \text{ matrix} &= N \times N = N^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Self-Interfaces (Diagonal):**

The diagonal cells $(i, i)$ represent the function *interfacing with itself* — these are **not external interfaces**; they are internal implementation details of the function.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of self-interfaces (diagonal)} &= N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Self-Interfaces from Total Cells:**

The remaining cells represent **all possible external interfaces**, including both inbound and outbound directions separately.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{External interface slots} &= N^2 - N \\
&= N(N - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for Symmetry (Most Engineering Interfaces are Bi-Directional or Uni-Directional):**

**Case A — Unidirectional interfaces (asymmetric):** Each ordered pair $(i, j)$ with $i \neq j$ is a distinct interface.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{uni}} = N^2 - N = N(N - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B — Bidirectional interfaces (symmetric):** The interface from $i$ to $j$ is the *same* as from $j$ to $i$, so we divide by 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{bi}} &= \frac{N^2 - N}{2} \\
&= \frac{N(N - 1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — General Engineering Rule:**

For most practical KTU problems, the **unidirectional** count is the safe answer (and is more conservative — it gives the *maximum* interfaces to manage).

$$
\boxed{I_{ext}^{max} = N(N - 1)}
$$

### Verification (Plug in $N = 5$, the KTU favorite):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ext}^{max} &= 5 \times (5 - 1) \\
&= 5 \times 4 \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bidirectional check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ext}^{bi} &= \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity check:** For a 5-function system, the maximum unidirectional external interfaces are **20**, and bidirectional are **10**. This matches the standard KTU answer key.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Leaf Functions in a Recursive Decomposition

> **Problem Statement:** A system function is decomposed recursively over $L$ levels, with each function branching into $k_i$ sub-functions at level $i$. Derive the total number of leaf functions.

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

**Step 1 — Level 1 (Top):** There is **1** top-level function (the system function itself).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_0 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Level 1 Decomposition:** Each of the $N_0$ functions splits into $k_1$ sub-functions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= N_0 \times k_1 = k_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Level 2 Decomposition:** Each of the $N_1$ functions splits into $k_2$ sub-functions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_2 &= N_1 \times k_2 = k_1 \cdot k_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — General Recursive Pattern:** At level $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_i &= \prod_{j=1}^{i} k_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Leaf Functions at Final Level $L$:**

$$
\boxed{N_L = \prod_{j=1}^{L} k_j}
$$

**Special Case — Uniform Branching (all $k_i = k$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_L &= k^L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example (KTU-Style Problem):

A satellite attitude control system is decomposed uniformly into $k = 4$ sub-functions over $L = 3$ levels.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_L &= k^L = 4^3 = 64 \text{ leaf functions}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why uncontrolled recursion is dangerous. A 3-level decomposition with 4-way branching already produces **64 leaves**, each needing a component. The KTU rule of **3-7 branches per level** keeps this manageable: $k=5, L=4 \Rightarrow 625$ leaves (too many); $k=4, L=3 \Rightarrow 64$ leaves (acceptable).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Weighted Trade Study Scoring — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem Statement:** A systems engineering team must select between **3 candidate architectures** (A, B, C) for an autonomous drone delivery system, evaluated against **4 criteria**: Cost (C1), Reliability (C2), Payload Capacity (C3), and Maintainability (C4). Compute the optimal architecture.

### Step 1 — Define Weights (Stakeholder-Approved)

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= 0.30 \text{ (Cost — most important)} \\
w_2 &= 0.25 \text{ (Reliability)} \\
w_3 &= 0.25 \text{ (Payload)} \\
w_4 &= 0.20 \text{ (Maintainability)} \\
\text{Check: } \sum w_i &= 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Score Each Alternative (Scale 1-10, Higher is Better)

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Alt A | Alt B | Alt C |
|-----------|----------------|-------|-------|-------|
| C1: Cost (lower=better, score inverted) | 0.30 | 7 | 9 | 5 |
| C2: Reliability | 0.25 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| C3: Payload Capacity | 0.25 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| C4: Maintainability | 0.20 | 7 | 6 | 8 |

### Step 3 — Compute Weighted Score for Each Alternative

**Alternative A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= w_1 \cdot s_{A1} + w_2 \cdot s_{A2} + w_3 \cdot s_{A3} + w_4 \cdot s_{A4} \\
&= (0.30)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(7) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.50 + 1.40 \\
&= 7.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(6) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.75 + 2.00 + 1.20 \\
&= 7.65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30)(5) + (0.25)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(8) \\
&= 1.50 + 2.25 + 1.75 + 1.60 \\
&= 7.10
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Select Optimal Alternative

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= 7.00, \quad S_B = 7.65, \quad S_C = 7.10 \\
A^* &= \arg\max_j S_j = \text{Alt B with score } 7.65
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity Analysis

**Perturbation:** Increase $w_1$ (Cost weight) by 10%: $w_1' = 0.33$, redistribute $0.03$ from $w_3$: $w_3' = 0.22$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A' &= (0.33)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.22)(6) + (0.20)(7) \\
&= 2.31 + 2.00 + 1.32 + 1.40 = 7.03 \\
S_B' &= (0.33)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.22)(8) + (0.20)(6) \\
&= 2.97 + 1.75 + 1.76 + 1.20 = 7.68 \\
S_C' &= (0.33)(5) + (0.25)(9) + (0.22)(7) + (0.20)(8) \\
&= 1.65 + 2.25 + 1.54 + 1.60 = 7.04
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** B remains optimal. Decision is **robust** to ±10% weight variation. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Architecture to System Quality Attributes

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Module 1 explicitly requires students to articulate how architectural choices impact system quality. The following matrix is a high-yield study tool.

| Quality Attribute | Best Architecture | Worst Architecture | Why |
|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|-----|
| **Performance (latency)** | Monolithic, pipe-and-filter | Microservices (high overhead) | Fewer hops = lower latency |
| **Scalability** | Microservices, SoS | Monolith | Independent scaling units |
| **Reliability (MTBF)** | Modular with redundancy | Monolith | Fault isolation |
| **Maintainability** | Layered, microservices | Tightly-coupled | Decoupled change impact |
| **Security** | Layered (defense-in-depth) | Flat | Multiple barriers |
| **Modifiability** | Modular, open-standards | Proprietary, locked-in | Swappable components |
| **Interoperability** | Service-Oriented, publish-subscribe | Hard-coded interfaces | Standard contracts |
| **Testability** | Modular, layered | Highly-interdependent | Isolated unit tests |
| **Cost (NRE)** | Reuse, COTS-based | Custom, from-scratch | Engineering effort |
| **Time-to-Market** | COTS + integration | Full custom development | Mature components |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, this matrix can earn you **3-4 marks** if used as a supporting diagram in your design rationale answer.

---

## 3.5 Algorithmic Derivation: Functional-to-Physical Allocation Procedure

> **Problem Statement:** Given a set of $F$ functions and a set of $P$ physical realizers, derive a systematic allocation procedure that satisfies functional completeness and physical realizability.

### Step-by-Step Procedure:

**Step 1 — Function Inventory:** List all $F$ leaf functions $\{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_F\}$.

**Step 2 — Realizer Inventory:** List all $P$ available physical realizers $\{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_P\}$ with their capability vectors $\vec{c}_p$ and constraint vectors $\vec{k}_p$ (mass, power, volume, cost).

**Step 3 — Capability Matching Matrix:** Construct an $F \times P$ matrix $M$ where $M_{ij} = 1$ if $r_j$ can realize $f_i$, else 0.

**Step 4 — Constraint Check:** For each function $f_i$ requiring a specific realizer (e.g., "must be software"), filter the realizer list.

**Step 5 — Optimization Assignment (Greedy Algorithm):** Assign each function to the realizer that:
- (a) has the lowest unused capacity after assignment,
- (b) satisfies all constraints, and
- (c) minimizes total coupling between realizers.

**Step 6 — Verification:** Confirm that every function is assigned to exactly one realizer (one-to-one mapping) and no realizer exceeds its capacity.

### Mathematical Formulation (Simplified):

Let $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ indicate whether function $i$ is assigned to realizer $j$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize: } \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{F} \sum_{j=1}^{P} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij} \\
\text{Subject to: } \quad & \sum_{j=1}^{P} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \quad \text{(each function assigned exactly once)} \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{F} a_{ij} \cdot x_{ij} \leq K_j \quad \forall j \quad \text{(capacity constraint)} \\
& x_{ij} \leq M_{ij} \quad \forall i,j \quad \text{(capability constraint)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $c_{ij}$ = cost of assigning $f_i$ to $r_j$
- $a_{ij}$ = capacity consumed by $f_i$ on $r_j$
- $K_j$ = total capacity of $r_j$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is a classic **Generalized Assignment Problem (GAP)**, NP-hard in general. In KTU exams, you do NOT need to solve the optimization — you need to **describe the procedure** and **interpret the constraints**. KTU rewards conceptual correctness over algorithmic complexity here.

## 4.8 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Fallback Diagram for Complex Systems)

For highly complex systems (e.g., a satellite constellation, a smart city, a hospital information system), where a single diagram cannot capture all interactions, the following **block-level functional architecture** is recommended:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT[EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT]
        E1[USERS]
        E2[OPERATORS]
        E3[REGULATORS]
    end
    
    subgraph SYS[CORE SYSTEM]
        S1[INPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        S2[PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM]
        S3[STORAGE SUBSYSTEM]
        S4[OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        S5[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
    end
    
    subgraph SUP[SUPPORTING INFRASTRUCTURE]
        SU1[POWER]
        SU2[NETWORK]
        SU3[MAINTENANCE]
    end
    
    E1 --> S1
    E2 --> S5
    E3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S2 --> S4
    S5 --> S2
    S5 --> S4
    SU1 --> S1
    SU1 --> S2
    SU1 --> S3
    SU1 --> S4
    SU1 --> S5
    SU2 --> S2
    SU2 --> S3
    SU2 --> S4
    SU3 --> SYS
```

> **Interpretation:** This **three-layer architecture** (External, Core, Supporting) is the standard structure for any complex engineering system. KTU 2024 Module 1 covers this as a **block-level functional flow**, which is the *recommended* approach when full physical or behavioral detail would exceed exam answer space.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
