> **Block Diagram (must draw, 2 marks):** see Section 4.2.

#### Part (b) — ADC SNR and Nyquist Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Theoretical SNR of 16-bit ADC**

$$
SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \, dB
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist Criterion Check**

$$
f_{max} = 200 \, Hz, \quad f_s = 1000 \, Hz
$$

$$
f_{Nyquist} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 \, Hz
$$

$$
f_s = 1000 \, Hz \;\; \geq \;\; 2 f_{max} = 400 \, Hz \;\; \checkmark
$$

The Nyquist criterion is **satisfied**.

**Step 3 — Anti-Alias Filter Specification**

To prevent aliasing from vibration harmonics above 200 Hz, a 2nd-order low-pass filter is recommended:

- **Type:** 2nd-order Butterworth (maximally flat passband).
- **Cut-off frequency:** Choose $f_c = 250 \, Hz$ (i.e., $1.25 \times f_{max}$, giving 50 Hz transition band below $f_{Nyquist}$).
- **Roll-off:** −40 dB/decade (12 dB/octave).

**Step 4 — Quantitative Justification**

- At 200 Hz, the desired signal passes with ≤3 dB attenuation (Butterworth tolerance).
- At 500 Hz (Nyquist), the filter attenuates by:

$$
A = 20 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{500}{250}\right)^2\right] = 20 \log_{10}(4) \approx 12 \, dB
$$

> This is a *minimum* attenuation. A 4th-order Butterworth with $f_c = 250 \, Hz$ would give 24 dB at 500 Hz — recommended for drone vibration-rich environments.

**Step 5 — Final Comment**

- The 16-bit ADC has 98 dB SNR, theoretically resolving $3.3 / 65536 = 50.4 \, \mu V$ LSB.
- Combined with the anti-alias filter, the IMU is well-suited for the drone's vibration band.

*(Award breakdown: SNR 1.5, Nyquist 1.5, filter design 2, quantitative attenuation 1, conclusion 1.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU 2024 Exam Checklist — Module 3 Sensor-Actuator Integration:**

- ☐ **Nyquist Theorem** is mandatory: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Memorize it; do *not* derive on the answer sheet.
- ☐ **SNR formula** for an ideal N-bit ADC is $6.02N + 1.76$ dB — for **N = 8, 10, 12, 14, 16** memorize the values **49.92, 61.96, 74.00, 86.04, 98.08 dB**.
- ☐ **Quantization step** of an ADC is $V_{FS} / 2^N$. Quantization RMS noise is $V_{LSB} / \sqrt{12}$.
- ☐ **Closed-loop latency** is a **sum of six terms** — never skip any. Drawing the block diagram automatically earns you 2–3 marks.
- ☐ **Four engineering axes** — bandwidth, latency, power, reliability — must appear in *every* design justification.
- ☐ **Protocol choice rules**: I²C and SPI for **on-board**; CAN-FD, EtherCAT, TSN, FlexRay for **field-bus**; 5G URLLC for **wireless**. UART is for **debug only**.
- ☐ **Sensor fusion** — *EKF* for IMU+GNSS, *complementary filter* for low-cost drones, *Kalman filter* for linear systems.
- ☐ **Time synchronization** — PTP (IEEE 1588) is the gold standard; PPS from GNSS for outdoor mobile CPS.
- ☐ **Actuator thermal model** — $T_{j,ss} = T_{amb} + P_{loss} R_{th,ja}$; always verify $T_{j,ss} \leq T_{j,max}$.
- ☐ **Anti-alias filter** must be **analog** and placed **before the ADC**, not after.
- ☐ **Hard real-time** CPS must satisfy $\tau_{loop} \leq T_{deadline}$ for *all* executions, not just the average.
- ☐ **Fault detection** — use **hardware redundancy** (3 sensors, voting) or **analytical redundancy** (parity/Kalman residual).
- ☐ **PWM duty cycle** $D = V_{cmd} / V_{supply}$; for bi-directional motors, use H-bridge.
- ☐ **Active sensors** generate their own signal (piezoelectric); **passive sensors** need excitation (thermistor).
- ☐ Always include **block diagrams and units** in your answer — KTU examiners reward *clarity*.

---

## 5.4 KTU 2024 Module-3 Cross-Reference Index

| Sub-topic | Bloom's Level | KTU Question Type |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor taxonomy | L1–L2 (Remember–Understand) | 2-mark direct |
| Nyquist theorem | L2–L3 (Understand–Apply) | 3-mark numerical |
| ADC SNR derivation | L3–L4 (Apply–Analyze) | 7-mark derivation |
| Closed-loop latency | L4 (Analyze) | 7-mark analytical |
| Protocol selection | L5 (Evaluate) | 7-mark design |
| Time synchronization | L3 (Apply) | 3-mark short |
| Sensor fusion (EKF) | L4–L5 (Analyze–Evaluate) | 14-mark design |

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *“In CPS, the loop is the system. If the loop is broken, the system is broken. The four axes keep the loop healthy.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Systems and Embedded Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT865 - SYSTEMS ENGINEERING AND INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Systems and Embedded Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Real-Time Systems (RTS) — Formal Academic Definition

A **Real-Time System** is a computer system whose correctness depends not only on the logical results of computation but also on the **time at which those results are produced**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, a real-time system is defined as:

> *“A system in which the response time to an event must occur within a strictly bounded time interval (called the deadline), or the system is considered to have failed.”*

**Key Formal Attributes:**
- **Determinism** — Bounded, predictable response times.
- **Timeliness** — Outputs are valid only within specific temporal windows.
- **Concurrency** — Multiple events/inputs must be handled in parallel.
- **Reliability** — Failure can cause catastrophic consequences (life, money, safety).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A real-time system is NOT necessarily a "fast" system. It is a **deterministic** system. Even a slow system can be real-time if its worst-case response time is **bounded and known in advance**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller" Model

Imagine an **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** guiding two aircraft to land:
- Aircraft A needs landing clearance by **6:00 PM sharp** (hard deadline).
- Aircraft B needs weather data update by **6:15 PM** (soft deadline — late is okay but not ideal).

If the ATC tells Aircraft A the landing clearance at 6:05 PM, **the result is logically correct** (cleared to land), but the system has **failed** because the time constraint was violated. This is the essence of real-time systems.

**In contrast**, a desktop computer downloading a file:
- Slow download is annoying, but **not** a system failure.
- Hence, it is **NOT** a real-time system.

---

## 1.3 Embedded Computing — Formal Definition

An **Embedded System** is a computing subsystem that is **integrated into a larger physical product** to perform a dedicated function, often with **real-time computing constraints**. The KTU 2024 Scheme defines it as:

> *“A microprocessor/microcontroller-based system designed to perform a specific function within a larger electromechanical or physical system, typically with strict constraints on power, cost, size, and timing.”*

**Three Pillars of Embedded Systems:**
1. **Hardware** — Microcontroller/Microprocessor, sensors, actuators, memory.
2. **Software** — Firmware, RTOS, device drivers, application logic.
3. **Real-Time Behavior** — Determinism in input → processing → output.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Washing Machine" Model

A modern washing machine is an **embedded system**:
- **Input**: User selects "Cotton, 40°C, Quick Wash."
- **Processing**: Microcontroller executes pre-programmed wash cycles.
- **Output**: Motor, water pump, heater activated at specific timing intervals.
- **Real-Time Constraint**: If the drain valve opens 5 minutes late, water overflows.

The washing machine does NOT run Windows or Linux; it runs **dedicated firmware** on a small microcontroller. This is the difference between a general-purpose computer and an **embedded computing system**.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Real-Time Systems

| Type | Definition | Consequence of Missed Deadline | Example |
|------|------------|-------------------------------|---------|
| **Hard Real-Time** | Deadline miss = system failure (catastrophic) | Loss of life / equipment | Airbag deployment, Pacemaker, Anti-lock Braking (ABS) |
| **Firm Real-Time** | Late result is discarded (no value) | Reduced quality of service | Video frame rendering (skip frame) |
| **Soft Real-Time** | Late result has degraded utility | User dissatisfaction, not failure | Web page load, Audio playback |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always explicitly classify a system as **Hard/Firm/Soft** and **justify** the classification using the consequence of deadline miss. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for correct classification with justification.

---

## 1.6 Core Design Metrics for Real-Time & Embedded Systems

The following are the **canonical performance metrics** evaluated in the KTU 2024 Scheme:

- **Response Time (R)** — Time from event arrival to response generation.
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** — Maximum possible execution time of a task under all possible input/state conditions.
- **Deadline (D)** — Latest acceptable completion time.
- **Jitter (J)** — Variation in response time between successive executions.
- **Throughput (T)** — Number of tasks completed per unit time.
- **Utilization (U)** — Fraction of CPU time consumed by the task set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The single most important metric in real-time systems is **WCET**. A system is real-time only if WCET ≤ Deadline for **every** task under **all** operating conditions. This is the formal **schedulability test**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Real-Time Embedded Systems

A real-time embedded system can be modeled as a **layered hierarchy** that the KTU 2024 Scheme describes as follows (from bottom to top):

### Layer 1 — Physical / Hardware Layer
- Sensors (input transducers): temperature, pressure, motion.
- Actuators (output transducers): motors, valves, LEDs.
- **Microcontroller/Processor**: ARM Cortex-M, AVR, RISC-V, DSP, FPGA.

### Layer 2 — System on Chip (SoC) Integration
- **CPU Core + Memory + Peripherals** on a single silicon die.
- Bus architectures: **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** for ARM-based SoCs.

### Layer 3 — Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Kernel
- **Scheduler** (Rate Monotonic, EDF, Priority-based).
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)**.
- **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)**: Semaphores, Message Queues, Mailboxes.
- **Timer Management** with hardware timer ticks.

### Layer 4 — Middleware & Application Layer
- Domain-specific software stacks (CAN stack for automotive, BLE stack for IoT).
- Application logic (control algorithms, state machines).

---

## 2.2 Real-Time Scheduling Theory — The Core Analytical Engine

### 2.2.1 Task Model

A real-time task $\tau_i$ is formally defined by the tuple:

$$\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i, U_i)$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)
- $T_i$ = Period of task
- $D_i$ = Relative deadline
- $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ = Utilization of task $i$

> [!NOTE]
> **Default KTU Assumption:** $D_i = T_i$ (implicit deadline). For **constrained deadline** systems, $D_i \leq T_i$. For **arbitrary deadlines**, $D_i > T_i$.

---

### 2.2.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Liu & Layland (1973)

**RMS** is the **optimal static-priority** scheduling algorithm for preemptive, independent, periodic tasks on a single processor.

**Core Rules:**
- Tasks with **shorter period** get **higher priority**.
- Priority assignment is **static** (does not change at runtime).
- Tasks are **preemptible**.
- Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources or precedence).

**Schedulability Test (Liu & Layland Bound):**

For a task set of $n$ periodic tasks, RMS guarantees schedulability if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

As $n \to \infty$, the bound converges to **ln(2) ≈ 0.693** (≈ 69.3%).

| n | RMS Utilisation Bound $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ |
|---|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | 1.000 |
| 2 | 0.828 |
| 3 | 0.780 |
| 4 | 0.757 |
| 5 | 0.743 |
| 10 | 0.718 |
| ∞ | 0.693 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Trap:** The RMS bound is a **sufficient but NOT necessary** condition. A task set with $U = 0.80$ may still be schedulable. For exact test, use the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** iterative equation (covered in Section 3).

---

### 2.2.3 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — Dynamic Priority

**EDF** is the **optimal dynamic-priority** scheduling algorithm. It guarantees schedulability on a single processor **if and only if**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$$

EDF can achieve **100% CPU utilization**, but at the cost of higher runtime overhead due to dynamic priority calculation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Per-task CPU utilization | Utilization calculation |
| 2 | $U_{total} = \sum U_i$ | Total system utilization | Schedulability check |
| 3 | $U_{RMS} \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | RMS sufficient test | Static priority systems |
| 4 | $U_{EDF} \leq 1$ | EDF exact test | Dynamic priority systems |
| 5 | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | Response Time Analysis iteration | Exact schedulability test |
| 6 | $R_i \leq D_i$ | RTA schedulability condition | Feasibility verification |
| 7 | $\text{Slack} = D_i - R_i$ | Slack time / laxity | Buffer analysis |
| 8 | $\text{Priority Inversion Time} \leq (n-1) \cdot C_{max}$ | Bounded priority inversion | Concurrency analysis |
| 9 | $\text{Interrupt Latency} = t_{recog} + t_{save} + t_{ISR} + t_{restore}$ | ISR timing breakdown | Latency calculation |
| 10 | $f_{tick} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{Prescaler \cdot Period}$ | RTOS tick frequency derivation | Timer configuration |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule Compliance:** All absolute values in formulas are written using $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ to avoid markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.4 Interrupt Handling in Embedded Real-Time Systems

**Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)** are the primary mechanism for handling asynchronous events in embedded real-time systems.

**ISR Execution Flow:**
1. **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** asserted by peripheral hardware.
2. CPU completes current instruction.
3. **Interrupt Recognition Time** ($t_{recog}$): CPU detects and prioritizes the IRQ.
4. **Context Save Time** ($t_{save}$): Registers pushed onto stack.
5. **ISR Execution** ($t_{ISR}$): Interrupt handler runs.
6. **Context Restore Time** ($t_{restore}$): Registers popped from stack.
7. **Return to Preempted Task**.

**Total Interrupt Latency:**

$$L_{ISR} = t_{recog} + t_{save} + t_{ISR} + t_{restore}$$

**Key Principles for Real-Time ISR Design:**
- **Keep ISRs short and fast** — defer heavy processing to "deferred procedure calls" (DPCs) or task-level handlers.
- **Disable nesting** to prevent unbounded latency (unless hardware supports prioritized nested interrupts).
- **Avoid blocking calls** (semaphores, malloc) inside ISRs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The cumulative interrupt latency must be **less than the tightest deadline** in the system. For a pacemaker, this is in the order of **<1 ms**. For ABS braking, **<10 ms**.

---

## 2.5 Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) — Concept & Examples

An **RTOS** is an operating system designed to handle real-time applications with bounded latency. Popular RTOS in the KTU industrial context include:

| RTOS | Domain | Key Feature |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **FreeRTOS** | IoT, MCUs | Open source, small footprint |
| **VxWorks** | Aerospace, Defense | DO-178C certified |
| **QNX** | Automotive (BlackBerry) | Microkernel, high reliability |
| **RTLinux** | Industrial control | Hard real-time Linux variant |
| **Zephyr** | Embedded IoT | Linux Foundation, scalable |

**Critical Distinction: RTOS vs General-Purpose OS (GPOS)**

| Parameter | RTOS | GPOS (Windows/Linux) |
|-----------|------|----------------------|
| Scheduling | Priority + preemption, deterministic | Fair share / time-sharing |
| Latency | Bounded, microseconds | Variable, milliseconds-seconds |
| Footprint | Kilobytes | Gigabytes |
| Use Case | Embedded control | Desktop / server |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility & Real-World Deployment

Real-time and embedded systems are the **invisible backbone of modern engineering**:

- **Automotive:** 100+ ECUs in a modern car, communicating via **CAN/LIN/FlexRay** bus.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control systems (fly-by-wire) running on **ARINC 653** partitioned RTOS.
- **Medical:** Infusion pumps, MRI machines, robotic surgery (da Vinci system).
- **Industrial:** SCADA, PLCs, robotic assembly lines.
- **Consumer IoT:** Smart thermostats, wearables, smart home hubs.
- **Defense:** Missile guidance, radar signal processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Project Tip:** When designing a real-time embedded system, the engineering trade-off triangle is **(Performance, Power, Cost)**. Always justify your choices against this triangle in viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — RMS Schedulability Analysis

### Problem Statement
Consider a real-time system with **three periodic tasks** with the following parameters:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 |

Determine if the task set is schedulable under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**.

---

### Step 1 — Assign RMS Priorities

RMS rule: **Shorter period → Higher priority**.

$$T_1 = 4 < T_2 = 6 < T_3 = 10$$

Therefore priority order: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Task Utilization

$$U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250$$

$$U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333$$

$$U_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.300$$

---

### Step 3 — Total Utilization

$$U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883$$

---

### Step 4 — Apply Liu & Layland RMS Bound

For $n = 3$ tasks, the sufficient bound is:

$$U_{bound}(n=3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$$

Let us evaluate this expression step by step.

$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

$$2^{1/3} - 1 \approx 0.2599$$

$$3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797$$

---

### Step 5 — Decision

$$U_{total} = 0.883 > U_{bound} = 0.780$$

The **sufficient condition FAILS**. However, since RMS bound is only sufficient (not necessary), we must perform **exact Response Time Analysis (RTA)** to confirm.

---

### Step 6 — Exact Response Time Analysis (RTA)

The RTA iterative equation is:

$$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

Where $hp(i)$ denotes the set of tasks with **higher priority** than $\tau_i$.

**For $\tau_3$ (lowest priority):** $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$

Initialize: $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$ ms.

**Iteration 1:**

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 9 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + 3 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Iteration 5:**

$$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2$$

$$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + 3 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Convergence Check:** $R_3^{(4)} = R_3^{(5)} = 10$ ms → Fixed point reached.

---

### Step 7 — Schedulability Decision

$$R_3 = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{vs} \quad D_3 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

Since $R_3 \leq D_3$, the task set **IS schedulable** under RMS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lesson:** This example is a classic case where the Liu-Layland sufficient bound **fails** (0.883 > 0.780), but the system is still **schedulable**. This is why KTU examiners love RTA problems — they test deeper understanding.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — EDF Schedulability

Same task set, now under **EDF**.

**EDF Schedulability Condition:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$$

**Computation:**

$$U_{total} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883 \leq 1$$

Therefore the task set is **schedulable under EDF** with 11.7% CPU slack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** EDF always succeeds if $U \leq 1$. The lower the $U$, the more **engineering margin** available for future task additions or transient overload handling.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Interrupt Latency Calculation

### Problem
An ARM Cortex-M3 microcontroller has the following parameters:
- Clock frequency: $f_{CPU} = 72$ MHz
- Interrupt recognition time: 12 clock cycles
- Context save (push): 8 cycles
- ISR execution: 45 cycles
- Context restore (pop): 8 cycles

**Calculate total interrupt latency in microseconds.**

---

### Step 1 — Total Clock Cycles

$$N_{total} = 12 + 8 + 45 + 8 = 73 \text{ cycles}$$

---

### Step 2 — Clock Period

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s}$$

---

### Step 3 — Total Latency

$$L = N_{total} \times T_{clk} = \frac{73}{72 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s}$$

$$L = 1.0139 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.014 \, \mu s$$

---

### Step 4 — Interpretation

$$L \approx 1.014 \, \mu s$$

This is well within **microsecond-level** real-time budgets suitable for motor control, automotive sensors, and high-speed data acquisition.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always include **units** in your final answer. Examiners deduct marks for unit-less numerical answers in latency calculations.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Priority Inversion Analysis

### Problem
Three tasks with priorities: $\tau_H$ (high), $\tau_M$ (medium), $\tau_L$ (low).
- $\tau_L$ acquires a mutex (shared resource) and gets preempted.
- $\tau_H$ becomes ready, preempts $\tau_L$, tries to acquire mutex → **BLOCKS**.
- $\tau_M$ preempts... $\tau_M$ runs unboundedly while $\tau_H$ waits.

This is the classic **Unbounded Priority Inversion Problem**.

**Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**
- $\tau_L$ temporarily **inherits** the priority of $\tau_H$ while holding the mutex.
- $\tau_M$ cannot preempt $\tau_L$ now (since $\tau_L$'s effective priority > $\tau_M$).
- $\tau_L$ releases the mutex, then resumes normal priority.
- $\tau_H$ acquires the mutex and runs.

**Bounded Priority Inversion Bound:**

$$T_{inv,max} \leq (n-1) \cdot C_{max}$$

Where $n$ is the number of lower-priority tasks and $C_{max}$ is their max execution time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of a Real-Time Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP1[Application Layer - Control Algorithms]
    APP2[Middleware - Communication Stacks]
    RTOS1[RTOS Kernel - Scheduler]
    RTOS2[ISR - Interrupt Service Routines]
    RTOS3[IPC - Semaphores Queues Mailboxes]
    HW1[SoC - CPU Memory Peripherals]
    HW2[Physical Sensors and Actuators]
    APP1 --> APP2
    APP2 --> RTOS1
    RTOS1 --> RTOS2
    RTOS1 --> RTOS3
    RTOS3 --> HW1
    HW1 --> HW2
```

---

## 4.2 RMS Scheduling Timeline Gantt Chart

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic Scheduling Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section High Priority
    Task1 First Instance :a1, 0, 1
    Task1 Second Instance :a2, 4, 1
    Task1 Third Instance :a3, 8, 1
    section Medium Priority
    Task2 First Instance :b1, 1, 2
    Task2 Second Instance :b2, 7, 2
    section Low Priority
    Task3 First Instance :c1, 3, 3
    Task3 Second Instance :c2, 9, 1
```

---

## 4.3 Interrupt Service Routine Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Peripheral Generates IRQ] --> B[CPU Finishes Current Instruction]
    B --> C[Interrupt Recognition]
    C --> D[Context Save Registers to Stack]
    D --> E[Execute ISR Handler]
    E --> F{More Pending Interrupts?}
    F -->|Yes| C
    F -->|No| G[Context Restore from Stack]
    G --> H[Return to Preempted Task]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Real-Time Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Input] --> CTRL[Controller Microcontroller]
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuator Motor Valve]
    ACT --> PROC[Physical Process Plant]
    PROC --> SENS[Sensor Feedback]
    SENS --> CTRL
    SENS -.->|Interrupt| ISR[ISR Handler]
    ISR --> CTRL
```

---

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Hard vs Soft Real-Time Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What happens if deadline is missed?}
    Q1 -->|Catastrophic Loss of Life| H[Hard Real-Time]
    Q1 -->|Result Discarded No Value| F[Firm Real-Time]
    Q1 -->|Quality Degraded User Annoyed| S[Soft Real-Time]
    H --> EX1[Example: Airbag Pacemaker]
    F --> EX2[Example: Video Frame Skip]
    S --> EX3[Example: Web Page Load]
```

---

## 4.6 Priority Inheritance Protocol Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Low Priority Task L Acquires Mutex] --> P2[High Priority Task H Requests Mutex]
    P2 --> P3[Task L Inherits High Priority]
    P3 --> P4[Medium Task M Cannot Preempt L]
    P4 --> P5[Task L Releases Mutex]
    P5 --> P6[Task H Acquires Mutex and Runs]
    P6 --> P7[Task L Resumes Original Priority]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Hard Real-Time and Soft Real-Time Systems with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Deadline | Strict, must be met always | Flexible, late acceptable |
| Consequence of miss | System failure, may cause loss of life | Performance degradation |
| Examples | Airbag controller, Pacemaker, ABS | Web browsing, Audio streaming |

*Examiners' distribution: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for examples.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET). Why is it the most critical parameter in real-time system design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** WCET is the maximum possible time a task can take to execute under all possible combinations of input data, system state, and execution path.

**Why Critical (2 marks):**
1. **Schedulability Verification** — A real-time system is feasible only if WCET ≤ Deadline for every task.
2. **Worst-Case Design Principle** — Real-time systems must be engineered for the **worst case**, not the average case. Optimistic estimation can cause deadline misses in production.
3. **Hardware Dependency** — WCET depends on CPU clock, cache behavior, memory access, and pipeline stalls. Accurate WCET estimation requires static analysis tools (e.g., aiT, OTAWA).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm. State and derive the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** for $n$ periodic tasks. For $n = 5$ tasks, compute the bound.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a real-time system with the following three tasks:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 10 |
| $\tau_3$ | 1 | 20 |

Determine if the task set is schedulable under **RMS** using both the **sufficient bound test** and **exact Response Time Analysis (RTA)**.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — RMS Algorithm and Bound Derivation

**RMS Definition (2 marks):**
Rate Monotonic Scheduling is a **static-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm for periodic real-time tasks. Tasks with **shorter periods** are assigned **higher priorities**. The priority is fixed at design time and does not change at runtime.

**Algorithm Properties (1 mark):**
- Optimal among all static-priority algorithms.
- Assumes tasks are independent, preemptible, and have implicit deadlines ($D_i = T_i$).
- No shared resources or precedence constraints.

**Liu & Layland Bound Derivation (3 marks):**

For a task set to be schedulable under RMS, the total utilization must satisfy:

$$U \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

The bound arises from the **critical instant** analysis: the worst-case response time of the lowest-priority task occurs when **all higher-priority tasks arrive simultaneously** at time $t = 0$ (critical instant). For $n$ tasks, the maximum achievable utilization under RMS at the critical instant yields the bound $n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$.

**Asymptotic Limit:** As $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

**Computation for $n = 5$ (1 mark):**

$$U_{bound}(5) = 5 \cdot (2^{1/5} - 1)$$

$$2^{1/5} = \sqrt[5]{2} \approx 1.1487$$

$$2^{1/5} - 1 \approx 0.1487$$

$$U_{bound}(5) = 5 \times 0.1487 = 0.7435 \approx 74.35\%$$

---

#### Part (b) — Schedulability Analysis

**Step 1 — Priority Assignment (1 mark):**
$$T_1 = 5 < T_2 = 10 < T_3 = 20$$
Priority: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.

**Step 2 — Total Utilization (1 mark):**

$$U = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{1}{20} = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.050 = 0.450$$

**Step 3 — Liu-Layland Test (1 mark):**
For $n = 3$, $U_{bound} = 0.780$.
$$U = 0.450 \leq 0.780 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{SUFFICIENT CONDITION SATISFIED}$$

**Step 4 — RTA for Confirmation (4 marks):**

**For $\tau_3$ (lowest priority):** $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$

$$R_3^{(0)} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 1 + 1 + 2 = 4 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 1 + 1 + 2 = 4 \text{ ms}$$

**Convergence** at $R_3 = 4$ ms.

**Decision:** $R_3 = 4 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = 20 \text{ ms}$ → **SCHEDULABLE** with abundant slack.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **architectural layers** of a real-time embedded system. Describe the role of **RTOS scheduler, ISR, and IPC** in the kernel layer.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**. How does it solve the **unbounded priority inversion** problem? State the bounded priority inversion limit.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Architectural Layers (7 marks)

The KTU 2024 scheme describes a real-time embedded system as a **four-layer hierarchy**:

**Layer 1 — Hardware/Physical Layer (2 marks):**
- **Sensors** (input): Temperature, pressure, accelerometer, Lidar.
- **Actuators** (output): DC motors, solenoids, LEDs, valves.
- **Microcontroller/Processor**: ARM Cortex-M, AVR, RISC-V, DSP.
- **SoC integration**: CPU + memory + peripherals (UART, SPI, I2C, ADC, PWM).

**Layer 2 — RTOS Kernel (3 marks):**
- **Scheduler**: Implements RMS, EDF, or priority-based preemptive scheduling. Decides **which task runs next** based on priority and deadline.
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISR)**: Hardware-triggered handlers with the **highest priority** in the system. Bounded execution time is critical.
- **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)**: Semaphores (binary, counting), mutexes, message queues, mailboxes, event flags. Used for synchronization and data exchange between tasks.

**Layer 3 — Middleware (1 mark):**
Protocol stacks: CAN, Modbus, TCP/IP, BLE, USB. Decouples application from hardware.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (1 mark):**
Domain-specific control logic, state machines, control algorithms (PID, fuzzy, MPC).

---

#### Part (b) — Priority Inheritance Protocol (7 marks)

**Priority Inversion Problem (2 marks):**
Occurs when a **high-priority task H** is forced to wait for a **low-priority task L** to release a shared resource (mutex), while **medium-priority tasks M** preempt L. This makes H wait for an **unbounded** amount of time — violating real-time guarantees.

**PIP Solution (3 marks):**
The Priority Inheritance Protocol temporarily **elevates** the priority of L to match the highest-priority task waiting on the resource. This prevents M from preempting L, allowing L to finish quickly and release the mutex.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.6 above for the PIP flow.

**Bounded Priority Inversion Limit (1 mark):**
Under PIP, the blocking time is bounded by:

$$T_{block,max} \leq (n-1) \cdot C_{max}$$

Where $n$ is the number of lower-priority tasks and $C_{max}$ is the maximum critical section length.

**Real-World Use:** PIP is implemented in **FreeRTOS** (configUSE_MUTEXES + configUSE_RECURSIVE_MUTEXES), **VxWorks**, and **Linux PREEMPT_RT** patches.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Real-Time Systems Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing utilization with throughput.** Utilization $U$ is a fraction (0–1), not a count. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit confusion.
>
> 2. **Using the RMS sufficient bound as a NECESSARY condition.** Writing "The system is NOT schedulable" simply because $U > 0.693$ is **wrong**. Always follow up with RTA.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in RTA iterations. The RTA equation **must** use ceiling of $(R_i / T_j)$, not floor or integer division. This is the #1 error KTU examiners penalize.
>
> 4. **Not stating the convergence condition** in RTA. You must explicitly state "$R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$, iteration converged" before drawing conclusions.
>
> 5. **Confusing deadline $D_i$ with period $T_i$.** When the problem states $D_i \neq T_i$, use $D_i$ in the RTA termination condition $R_i \leq D_i$, not $T_i$.
>
> 6. **Missing units** in interrupt latency or throughput answers. Always state "ms", "$\mu s$", or "Mbps".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Real-time ≠ Fast.** A real-time system is **deterministic**, not necessarily high-speed.
- [ ] **Three classes:** Hard (catastrophic), Firm (discarded), Soft (degraded).
- [ ] **WCET** is the most critical metric; it must be bounded and known in advance.
- [ ] **RMS** = static priority, optimal for static, sufficient bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$, limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- [ ] **EDF** = dynamic priority, exact bound $U \leq 1$, optimal for dynamic.
- [ ] **RTA** iteration: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)}/T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$.
- [ ] **ISR latency** = recognition + context save + ISR + context restore.
- [ ] **Priority Inversion** solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**.
- [ ] **RTOS** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX) differs from **GPOS** (Windows, Linux) in latency and footprint.
- [ ] **Embedded systems** integrate hardware, firmware, and real-time behavior into a dedicated product.
- [ ] **Critical Instant** in RMS: all higher-priority tasks arrive at $t=0$ simultaneously.
- [ ] **WCET analysis** uses static analysis tools (aiT, OTAWA, AbsInt).
- [ ] **RTOS tick frequency** derivation: $f_{tick} = f_{CPU} / (Prescaler \times Period)$.
- [ ] **Industrial examples**: ABS, airbag, pacemaker, drone flight controller, SCADA, PLC.
- [ ] **Common bus protocols** for embedded: CAN (automotive), LIN (low-cost automotive), FlexRay, I2C, SPI, UART.
- [ ] **Jitter** is the variation in response time; minimize jitter for control loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for Real-Time Systems:**
> *"In a real-time system, a correct answer at the wrong time is a wrong answer. Determinism beats speed."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networking and Communication Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT865 - SYSTEMS ENGINEERING AND INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Networking and Communication Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Networking — Formal Definition
**Computer Networking** is the engineering discipline concerned with the design, deployment, integration, and management of interconnected computing systems that exchange data and share resources through a unified set of communication protocols. In the context of **Systems Engineering and Integration**, networking forms the *digital backbone* of any large-scale engineered system (aerospace, industrial automation, IoT, defense, smart-grid, vehicular systems), enabling distributed subsystems to communicate deterministically and reliably.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *network protocol* is a formal set of rules, conventions, and data structures that govern how devices transmit and receive information across a communication medium. Without protocols, heterogeneous systems cannot interoperate — a violation of the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** design philosophy.

### 1.2 Communication Protocol — Definition
A **Communication Protocol** is a contractually defined sequence of messages, signal formats, and state-transition rules agreed upon by two or more communicating entities. Protocols operate at multiple layers of abstraction — from the physical voltage transitions on a copper wire to the application-level semantics of an HTTP GET request.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" Model
Think of a network as the **national postal system**:
- **Sender (Host A)** writes a *letter* (the **data payload**).
- The letter is placed inside an **envelope** that has the destination address and return address written on it (**header information / protocol metadata**).
- The envelope goes to the **local post office** (the **physical layer**), then to a **regional sorting hub** (the **network layer**), and so on.
- At the destination, the envelope is delivered to **Host B**, which opens the letter.
- The *language* of the letter (English, Malayalam, JSON, XML) is the **application-layer protocol**.

> [!NOTE]
> Each layer in a network stack **adds its own envelope (header)** to the data and passes it down — a process known as **Encapsulation**. The reverse process at the receiver is **Decapsulation**.

### 1.4 Standardized Metrics Used Throughout Networking

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Rate / Bandwidth | $R$ or $B$ | **bits per second (bps)** | Bits transmitted per unit time |
| Latency / Delay | $D$ | **seconds (s)** or **ms** | Time taken for one bit/frame to travel source → destination |
| Throughput | $T$ | **bps** | Useful data delivered per second |
| Packet Loss Ratio | $PLR$ | **dimensionless (%)** | Lost packets / total packets |
| Jitter | $J$ | **ms** | Variation in inter-packet arrival time |
| Bit Error Rate | $BER$ | **dimensionless** | Errors per bit transmitted |

### 1.5 Why Networking Matters in Systems Integration
In KTU's *Systems Engineering* philosophy, every engineered product (a satellite, a car, a smart factory) is decomposed into **subsystems**. Networking is the *connective tissue* that:
1. Enables **distributed control** (e.g., ECU networks in automobiles — CAN/LIN/Ethernet).
2. Provides **telemetry and command pathways** for ground-station-to-satellite links.
3. Facilitates **SCADA and IIoT** integration in process industries.
4. Supports **deterministic real-time communication** in avionics (AFDX, ARINC 664).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The OSI 7-Layer Reference Model
The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model** (ISO/IEC 7498-1) is a conceptual framework that standardizes the functions of a telecommunication or computing system into **7 abstraction layers**. Each layer communicates *only* with its immediate peers (vertically) and adjacent layers (horizontally).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic (Top-Down):** **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**
> Layers 7 → 1: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical.

#### Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

**Layer 7 — Application Layer**
- Closest to the end-user. Provides network services to applications.
- Examples: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP, MQTT.
- Data unit: **Message**.

**Layer 6 — Presentation Layer**
- Handles data translation, encryption/decryption, compression.
- Examples: TLS/SSL, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII.
- Data unit: **Message** (formatted).

**Layer 5 — Session Layer**
- Manages sessions/connections between applications (establish, maintain, terminate).
- Examples: NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP.
- Data unit: **Message**.

**Layer 4 — Transport Layer**
- Provides end-to-end communication, error recovery, flow control, segmentation.
- Core protocols: **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) and **UDP** (unreliable, connectionless).
- Data unit: **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP).

**Layer 3 — Network Layer**
- Logical addressing and routing across multiple networks.
- Core protocol: **IP (IPv4 / IPv6)**, ICMP, OSPF, BGP.
- Data unit: **Packet**.

**Layer 2 — Data Link Layer**
- Node-to-node frame transfer, MAC addressing, error detection on the link.
- Sub-layers: **LLC** (Logical Link Control) and **MAC** (Media Access Control).
- Examples: Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP.
- Data unit: **Frame**.

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer**
- Transmits raw bits over a physical medium (copper, fiber, RF).
- Defines voltage levels, connector pinouts, modulation, bit synchronization.
- Data unit: **Bit / Symbol**.

### 2.2 The TCP/IP 4-Layer Model (Internet Protocol Suite)
The **TCP/IP model** (RFC 1122, RFC 791) is the practical implementation model used by the Internet. It collapses the 7-layer OSI into 4 layers:

| TCP/IP Layer | Maps to OSI Layers | Core Protocols | Data Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | 7, 6, 5 | HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP, MQTT | Message |
| Transport | 4 | TCP, UDP | Segment / Datagram |
| Internet | 3 | IP, ICMP, ARP | Packet |
| Link (Network Access) | 2, 1 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP | Frame / Bit |

### 2.3 Network Topologies
A **network topology** is the geometric arrangement of nodes and links.

- **Bus Topology** — Single backbone cable; all nodes share the medium. Simple but a single break disables the network.
- **Star Topology** — All nodes connect to a central hub/switch. Most common in LANs. Failure of the central node is catastrophic.
- **Ring Topology** — Each node connects to two neighbors; data circulates in one (or both) directions. Used in **Token Ring** and **FDDI**. Deterministic access.
- **Mesh Topology** — Every node connects to every other node. Used in **MANETs**, **WSNs**, and **tactical military networks**. High redundancy, high cost.
- **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology** — Hybrid of star and bus; used in large enterprise networks.
- **Hybrid Topology** — Mixture of two or more topologies.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Communication & Networking Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Use this **cheat sheet** as the single source of formulas for any numerical problem in this module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bit Rate from file size | $R = \dfrac{F \cdot 8}{T}$ | $F$ = file size in **bytes**, $T$ = time in **seconds** |
| 2 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless) |
| 3 | Nyquist Bit Rate (noiseless) | $R_{\max} = 2B \log_2 M$ | $M$ = signal levels, $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) |
| 4 | Propagation Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = distance, $v$ = propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber) |
| 5 | Transmission Delay | $t_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| 6 | End-to-End Delay | $D_{e2e} = t_p + t_t + t_q + t_p^{proc}$ | $t_q$ = queuing, $t_p^{proc}$ = nodal processing |
| 7 | Round Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2 \cdot t_p$ | (For symmetric link, ignoring processing) |
| 8 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \cdot t_p$ | Bits in flight; determines window size |
| 9 | Throughput (Stop-and-Wait) | $T_{S\&W} = \dfrac{L}{t_t + 2t_p + t_{ack}}$ | With ACK round-trip |
| 10 | Utilization (Stop-and-Wait) | $U = \dfrac{t_t}{t_t + 2t_p}$ | Efficiency; if $t_t \ll 2t_p$, utilization is poor |
| 11 | CRC Polynomial Degree | Detects all burst errors up to $r$ bits, $r$ = degree | Polynomial $G(x)$ of degree $r$ |
| 12 | Hamming Distance | Minimum bit-flips to change one valid codeword to another | Error detection: $d_{\min} \geq s+1$; Correction: $d_{\min} \geq 2t+1$ |
| 13 | Token Holding Time (Token Ring) | $THT = \dfrac{\text{Ring Latency}}{\text{Number of Stations}}$ | Used in IEEE 802.5 |
| 14 | Slotted Aloha Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $G$ = offered load (Erlangs); max at $G=1$, $S_{\max} = 1/e \approx 0.368$ |
| 15 | Pure Aloha Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S_{\max} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ |
| 16 | CSMA/CD Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$, where $a = t_p / t_t$ | Ethernet 802.3 |
| 17 | IPv4 Address Count | $N = 2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^9$ | Total addressable hosts (theoretical) |
| 18 | IPv6 Address Count | $N = 2^{128}$ | Astronomical; eliminates NAT need |
| 19 | Subnet Host Count | $H = 2^{h} - 2$ | $h$ = host bits; subtract 2 for network & broadcast |
| 20 | Manchester Encoding Bit Rate | $R_{Manchester} = \dfrac{R_{NRZ}}{2}$ | Each bit requires one transition |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aerospace & Avionics:** AFDX (Avionics Full-Duplex Switched Ethernet, ARINC 664) — deterministic Ethernet for flight-critical systems. Bandwidth = **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps** with bounded latency.
- **Automotive:** CAN bus (**1 Mbps max**), LIN (**19.2 kbps**), Automotive Ethernet (**100 Mbps – 1 Gbps**).
- **Industrial IoT:** Modbus TCP, PROFINET, EtherCAT, OPC-UA.
- **Space Communication:** CCSDS (Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems) protocols over S/X/Ka-band RF links.
- **Defense / Tactical:** Link 16 (MIL-STD-6016), Software Defined Radios (SDR).

### 2.6 Protocol Operation — Why & How
- **Why encapsulation?** Each layer *adds metadata* to satisfy a specific contract (e.g., network layer adds IP addresses, link layer adds MAC addresses). This separation of concerns is the **core design principle** of layered architecture.
- **Why TCP and UDP coexist?** TCP provides **reliability** (retransmission, ordering, flow control) at the cost of latency. UDP provides **low-latency, best-effort** delivery suitable for real-time voice/video/IoT.
- **Why CSMA/CD?** Ethernet's **Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection** lets nodes share a half-duplex medium fairly, with exponential backoff after collision.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental theorem of information theory** (Claude Shannon, 1948). It answers: *What is the maximum error-free data rate over a noisy channel?*

**Step 1 — Set up the problem.**
We have a communication channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ (dimensionless). We wish to find the maximum achievable data rate $C$ (bps).

**Step 2 — Start from the definition of information per symbol.**
For a noiseless channel with $M$ discrete signal levels, each symbol carries $\log_2 M$ bits. By the **Nyquist criterion**, the maximum symbol rate over a bandwidth-limited channel of $B$ Hz is $2B$ symbols/second (for a baseband channel, no ISI).

**Step 3 — Combine Nyquist rate with the noisy-channel limit.**
In a noisy channel, the number of *distinguishable* signal levels is constrained by the noise. Shannon showed that the effective number of distinguishable levels is approximately $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$ per dimension, yielding:

$$
\text{Bits per symbol (noisy)} = \log_2 \!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Nyquist's symbol rate.**
Multiplying the bits-per-symbol by the maximum symbol rate $2B$ gives the **Nyquist-Shannon formula**:

$$
C = 2B \log_2 \!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a bandpass channel, the prefactor may be $B$ rather than $2B$ depending on the modulation scheme (e.g., QAM uses both I and Q). The general form used in most textbooks is: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.

**Step 5 — Verify with units.**
$B$ has units of Hz (= symbols/second for a 1-symbol/Hz system). The logarithm is dimensionless. So $C$ has units of **bits/second** ✓.

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**
- If $S/N = 1000$ (30 dB) and $B = 3000$ Hz (telephone channel): $C = 3000 \log_2(1001) \approx 3000 \times 9.97 \approx 29903$ bps ≈ **30 kbps** — the historical modem limit.
- Doubling $B$ doubles $C$ (linearly).
- Doubling $S/N$ does **not** double $C$ (logarithmically).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stop-and-Wait Protocol Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define the scenario.**
A sender transmits one frame of $L$ bits at link rate $R$ (bps), waits for an acknowledgment (ACK), and then sends the next frame. This is **Stop-and-Wait ARQ**.

**Step 2 — Compute transmission time of the data frame.**

$$
t_t = \frac{L}{R} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute propagation delay to the receiver and back.**
For a one-way propagation delay of $t_p$ seconds, the ACK round-trip is $2 t_p$ seconds.

**Step 4 — Compute the total cycle time.**

$$
T_{cycle} = t_t + 2t_p + t_{ack} + t_{proc}
$$

For simplicity, ignore the ACK transmission time and processing delays:

$$
T_{cycle} \approx t_t + 2t_p
$$

**Step 5 — Compute utilization $U$ as the fraction of time the link carries useful data.**

$$
U = \frac{t_t}{t_t + 2t_p} = \frac{L/R}{L/R + 2 \cdot d/v} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

where the parameter $a$ (sometimes denoted $a = t_p / t_t$) captures the channel's *bandwidth-delay characteristic*:

$$
a = \frac{t_p}{t_t} = \frac{d/v}{L/R} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}
$$

**Step 6 — Analyse the result.**

- If $a \ll 1$ (small distance or small RTT relative to frame): $U \to 1$ (efficient).
- If $a \gg 1$ (satellite link, very high bandwidth): $U \to 0$ (terrible — most time is wasted waiting).

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example.**
A geostationary satellite link has $R = 1$ Mbps, $d = 36000$ km, $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $L = 1500$ bytes.

$$
t_p = \frac{36000 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.12 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
t_t = \frac{1500 \times 8}{10^6} = 0.012 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
U = \frac{12}{12 + 240} = \frac{12}{252} \approx 0.0476 = 4.76\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Only **4.76 %** link utilization! This is why **sliding-window protocols** (e.g., Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) and **TCP's windowing mechanism** exist — to keep the pipe full.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sliding Window Utilization

**Step 1 — Define window size $W$.**
The sender may transmit up to $W$ frames before waiting for an ACK.

**Step 2 — Compute the time to send $W$ frames.**

$$
T_{W} = W \cdot t_t
$$

**Step 3 — If $W \cdot t_t \geq 2 t_p + t_{ack}$, the link is kept fully busy.**

$$
U = 1 \quad \text{when} \quad W \geq \frac{2t_p}{t_t} + 1
$$

**Step 4 — If $W \cdot t_t < 2t_p$, the sender blocks waiting for the first ACK.**

$$
U = \frac{W \cdot t_t}{W \cdot t_t + 2t_p} = \frac{W}{W + 2a}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum window to avoid underutilization (satellite example).**

$$
W_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{2 \cdot 0.12}{0.012} \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil 20 \rceil + 1 = 21 \text{ frames}
$$

This is why **TCP window scaling** (RFC 1323) allows windows up to **1 GB** — for high-bandwidth × long-delay paths (the *long fat network* problem).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Subnetting and Host Count

**Step 1 — Convert IPv4 address to binary.**
A Class B network `172.16.0.0` uses the first 16 bits for network, leaving 16 bits for hosts.

**Step 2 — Apply a subnet mask.**
Suppose we use mask `255.255.240.0` (= `/20`). Then 4 bits are borrowed for subnetting, leaving $h = 12$ host bits.

**Step 3 — Compute the number of usable host addresses per subnet.**

$$
H = 2^{h} - 2 = 2^{12} - 2 = 4094
$$

The "-2" subtracts the **all-zeros** (network address) and **all-ones** (broadcast address) hosts.

**Step 4 — Number of subnets created.**
With 4 borrowed bits:

$$
N_{subnets} = 2^{4} = 16
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **modern CIDR** (Classless Inter-Domain Routing, RFC 4632), the all-zeros and all-ones subnets *are* usable. The "-2 rule" for subnets is deprecated.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection for Engineering Systems

| System Class | Critical Parameters | Selected Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flight Control Avionics | Determinism, fault tolerance, < 1 ms latency | AFDX (ARINC 664) | Bounded latency, dual-redundant |
| Automotive Powertrain | Real-time, EMI-robust, low cost | CAN 2.0B (1 Mbps) | CSMA/CR arbitration |
| Smart Grid SCADA | Reliability, security, legacy compat | DNP3 / IEC 61850 | Designed for substations |
| Industrial Robot Cell | Hard real-time, sync, cycle < 1 ms | EtherCAT | Sub-µs jitter |
| Satellite TT&C | Long delay, low BER | CCSDS over X-band | Space-grade error coding |
| IoT Sensor Mesh | Power efficiency, scale | MQTT over LoRaWAN | Lightweight pub/sub |
| Tactical Battlefield | Mobile, ad-hoc, anti-jam | Link 16 / MANET | Frequency hopping |
