### Step 6 — Final Discrete-Time State-Space Form

Define the **discretized system matrices**:

$$
A_d = e^{A T_s}, \qquad B_d = A^{-1} \left( e^{A T_s} - I \right) B
$$

Then the **discrete-time CPS dynamics** become:

$$
\boxed{\,x[k+1] = A_d \, x[k] + B_d \, u[k]\,}
$$

**Logic:** This is the canonical form used by every embedded controller in a CPS — it converts continuous physics into a form a digital computer can step through numerically.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Sampling Rate for a CPS (Nyquist)

### Given
A physical signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency content $f_{max}$ (in Hz).

### Step 1 — Statement of the Sampling Theorem
To reconstruct $x(t)$ losslessly from its samples, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply to a CPS Control Loop
A control loop operates at sampling rate $f_s = 1/T_s$. To track disturbances and reference changes of bandwidth $f_{bw}$:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{bw}
$$

### Step 3 — Practical Engineering Rule
Real engineers use a safety factor $\alpha$ (typically 5–10×):

$$
f_s \geq \alpha \cdot 2 f_{bw}, \qquad \alpha \in [5, 10]
$$

**Logic:** A higher sampling rate provides margin for computational delay, jitter, and anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CPS vs Related Paradigms

| Attribute | Embedded System | IoT | CPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary focus** | Single device computation | Connectivity & data | Closed-loop control |
| **Physical interaction** | Optional | Optional | **Mandatory & central** |
| **Real-time constraint** | Often | Rarely | **Almost always** |
| **Feedback loop** | Open-loop typical | Usually absent | **Tight, continuous** |
| **Network role** | Often local bus | Internet-centric | Heterogeneous, time-critical |
| **Safety-criticality** | Varies | Usually low | **Usually high** |
| **Mathematical model** | State machine / dataflow | Data pipeline | **Hybrid automaton / state-space** |
| **Engineering discipline** | CS / EE | CS / IT | **Multi-disciplinary (CS + EE + Mech + Control)** |
| **Design priority** | Functionality | Scalability, reach | **Determinism + correctness + safety** |
| **Example** | Microwave controller | Smart bulb | Autonomous vehicle, robotic surgery |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 7-mark comparison question between CPS and IoT almost always appears in module assessments. The **key differentiator** is the **closed-loop physical control** — IoT *observes and informs*, CPS *observes, decides, and physically acts*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Flow: Mapping a CPS to Its Functional Stages

$$
\text{Physical Process} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensor}} \text{Raw Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{A/D}} \text{Digital Sample} \xrightarrow{\text{Network}} \text{Cyber Processor}
$$

$$
\text{Cyber Processor} \xrightarrow{\text{Algorithm}} \text{Control Command} \xrightarrow{\text{Network}} \text{D/A} \xrightarrow{\text{Actuator}} \text{Physical Action}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Physical Action} \rightarrow \text{New Physical State} \rightarrow \text{Loop Closes}}
$$

This is the **functional abstraction** that any CPS engineer — whether designing a pacemaker or a satellite — uses as a mental model.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: CPS Three-Layer Architecture with Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePhys[Physical Layer]:::phys
    nodeSense[Sensors A to D]:::comm
    nodeNet1[Communication Network]:::comm
    nodeCyber[Cyber Layer Computation]:::cyber
    nodeNet2[Communication Network]:::comm
    nodeAct[Actuators D to A]:::comm
    nodeCtrl[Control Algorithm]:::cyber
    nodeEst[State Estimation]:::cyber
    nodeData[Data Logging]:::cyber

    nodePhys -- State Variables --> nodeSense
    nodeSense -- Digital Samples --> nodeNet1
    nodeNet1 -- Telemetry Data --> nodeEst
    nodeEst -- Estimated State --> nodeCtrl
    nodeCtrl -- Control Command --> nodeNet2
    nodeNet2 -- Actuation Signal --> nodeAct
    nodeAct -- Physical Action --> nodePhys
    nodeCtrl -- Event Records --> nodeData

    classDef phys fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef cyber fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#336633,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **orange block** is the physical process being controlled.
- The **green blocks** are the sensors, network, and actuators — the communication infrastructure.
- The **blue blocks** are the cyber layer: state estimation, control logic, and data logging.
- Arrows flow **clockwise** to depict the closed feedback loop.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: CPS Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlantBlock[Physical Plant Domain]
        nodePlant[Physical Process x of t]
        nodePlantEnv[Disturbances d of t]
    end

    subgraph SenseBlock[Sensing Subsystem]
        nodeS1[Sensor 1 Temperature]
        nodeS2[Sensor 2 Pressure]
        nodeS3[Sensor 3 Flow]
        nodeMux[Multiplexer]
        nodeADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    end

    subgraph CyberBlock[Cyber Processing Domain]
        nodeRTOS[Real Time Operating System]
        nodeCtrlAlg[Control Algorithm PID or MPC]
        nodeSaf[Safety Monitor]
        nodeLog[Telemetry Logger]
        nodeDAC[Command Formatter]
    end

    subgraph ActBlock[Actuation Subsystem]
        nodeDACA[Digital to Analog Converter]
        nodeA1[Actuator 1 Valve]
        nodeA2[Actuator 2 Motor]
        nodeA3[Actuator 3 Heater]
    end

    nodePlantEnv --> nodePlant
    nodePlant --> nodeS1
    nodePlant --> nodeS2
    nodePlant --> nodeS3
    nodeS1 --> nodeMux
    nodeS2 --> nodeMux
    nodeS3 --> nodeMux
    nodeMux --> nodeADC
    nodeADC --> nodeRTOS
    nodeRTOS --> nodeCtrlAlg
    nodeRTOS --> nodeSaf
    nodeRTOS --> nodeLog
    nodeCtrlAlg --> nodeDAC
    nodeSaf --> nodeDAC
    nodeDAC --> nodeDACA
    nodeDACA --> nodeA1
    nodeDACA --> nodeA2
    nodeDACA --> nodeA3
    nodeA1 --> nodePlant
    nodeA2 --> nodePlant
    nodeA3 --> nodePlant
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **Plant block** (top) contains the physical process and external disturbances.
- The **Sensing block** converts physical phenomena into digital samples.
- The **Cyber block** houses the RTOS, control algorithm, safety monitor, and logger — the "intelligence" of the CPS.
- The **Actuation block** translates commands back into physical actions.
- The loop closes: actuators alter the plant, sensors observe the new state.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: CPS Lifecycle in Systems Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeReq[Requirements Analysis]:::phase
    nodeMod[Modeling and Simulation]:::phase
    nodeDes[Hardware Software Co Design]:::phase
    nodeImp[Implementation]:::phase
    nodeInt[Integration]:::phase
    nodeVal[Validation and Verification]:::phase
    nodeDep[Deployment]:::phase
    nodeOps[Operations and Monitoring]:::phase
    nodeRet[Retirement]:::phase

    nodeReq --> nodeMod
    nodeMod --> nodeDes
    nodeDes --> nodeImp
    nodeImp --> nodeInt
    nodeInt --> nodeVal
    nodeVal --> nodeDep
    nodeDep --> nodeOps
    nodeOps --> nodeRet
    nodeOps -.feedback.-> nodeReq

    classDef phase fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
```

**Reading:** The CPS lifecycle mirrors the classical V-model of systems engineering. The dashed feedback arrow from **Operations** back to **Requirements** captures the **continuous evolution** characteristic of modern CPS — post-deployment telemetry drives new requirements (e.g., OTA updates, predictive maintenance).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define a Cyber-Physical System. List any four real-world examples of CPS.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A Cyber-Physical System is an integrated computational and physical system in which embedded computers and networks monitor, control, and coordinate the physical processes through continuous feedback loops operating under real-time constraints.

> **Four Real-World Examples:**
> 1. **Autonomous Vehicles** — sensor data → AI planner → steering/brake actuation
> 2. **Smart Grid** — distributed sensors balance supply and demand in real time
> 3. **Medical Devices** — insulin pumps adjust dosage based on continuous glucose sensing
> 4. **Industrial Robots** — programmable logic controllers coordinate arms in real time on a factory floor

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and the Internet of Things (IoT) in terms of three key parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CPS | IoT |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Loop** | Closed-loop with real-time physical actuation | Mostly open-loop; data collection and reporting |
| **Real-Time Constraint** | Hard real-time; deadline misses are failures | Soft real-time; delays degrade experience only |
| **Engineering Focus** | Determinism, safety, and control correctness | Connectivity, scalability, and data analytics |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer architecture** of a Cyber-Physical System. Describe the role of each layer in detail.

**Model Solution:**

A CPS is conventionally partitioned into three functional layers:

**1. Physical Layer:**
- Comprises the engineered or natural process under control (e.g., engine, chemical reactor, power grid).
- Characterized by continuous-time variables (temperature, pressure, position) governed by physical laws.
- Interacts with the environment and is subject to disturbances.

**2. Cyber Layer:**
- Consists of embedded processors, microcontrollers, servers, and software algorithms.
- Performs **sensing data fusion**, **state estimation**, **decision-making** (control laws, optimization, ML inference), and **actuation command generation**.
- Runs under a real-time operating system (RTOS) to ensure deterministic execution.

**3. Communication Layer:**
- Acts as the bridge between physical and cyber layers.
- Comprises **sensors** (A/D converters), **actuators** (D/A converters, power electronics), and **network protocols** (CAN, EtherCAT, TSN, 5G, MQTT).
- Carries telemetry from plant to controller and commands from controller to plant.

> The three layers form a **closed feedback loop**: physical state → sensing → communication → cyber computation → communication → actuation → altered physical state.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **key characteristics** that distinguish CPS from traditional embedded systems. Why is CPS considered the unifying framework for modern engineered systems?

**Model Solution:**

| Characteristic | Traditional Embedded System | CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical coupling** | Optional | Central to design |
| **Real-time guarantees** | Often soft | Usually hard |
| **Network role** | Peripheral | Core enabler |
| **Mathematical modeling** | Dataflow / state machine | Hybrid automata / state-space |
| **Safety-criticality** | Variable | Almost always safety-critical |
| **Cross-disciplinary** | Mostly EE + CS | EE + CS + Mechanical + Control + Domain |

**Why CPS is the unifying framework:**
- **Convergence of disciplines** — CPS forces integration of control theory, computer science, communications, and domain engineering.
- **Lifecycle management** — CPS demands continuous engineering (development → deployment → monitoring → updates), aligning with modern systems engineering.
- **Safety certification** — Standards like ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (aerospace), and IEC 61508 (industrial) all assume a CPS-style architecture.
- **Scalability** — From a single smart thermostat to a national power grid, the CPS abstraction scales uniformly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question B — INTERNAL CHOICE]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **end-to-end control loop** in a CPS with reference to a **smart irrigation system**. Identify the sensors, actuators, communication protocol, and control law.

**Model Solution:**

A **smart irrigation system** waters a farm based on real-time soil moisture, weather forecast, and crop type.

| CPS Element | Smart Irrigation Realization |
|---|---|
| **Physical Layer (Plant)** | Agricultural field with soil, crops, and water pumps |
| **Sensors** | Soil moisture probes, temperature sensors, rain gauge, humidity sensor |
| **Communication Layer** | LoRaWAN / Zigbee / 4G to cloud gateway; MQTT over TCP/IP |
| **Cyber Layer** | Edge controller (Raspberry Pi / Arduino) + cloud analytics |
| **Control Law** | Threshold-based: $u[k] = 1$ if $\theta < \theta_{dry}$, else $0$; OR PID-based: $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e + K_d \Delta e$ where $e = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{measured}$ |
| **Actuators** | Solenoid valves controlling water flow, motor-driven pumps |
| **Feedback** | New soil moisture reading closes the loop every $T_s$ seconds (e.g., $T_s = 60$ s) |

**End-to-End Flow:**
$$\text{Soil} \xrightarrow{\text{Probe}} \text{ADC} \xrightarrow{\text{LoRa}} \text{Cloud} \xrightarrow{\text{PID}} \text{Valve Command} \xrightarrow{\text{LoRa}} \text{Valve} \rightarrow \text{Water Flow} \rightarrow \text{New Soil State}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **discrete-time state-space representation** of a continuous-time CPS plant governed by $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ under Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) sampling at interval $T_s$.

**Model Solution:**

**(Detailed Step-by-Step — Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)**

**Key Results:**

Starting from the continuous-time state equation and applying the analytical solution:

$$
x(t) = e^{A(t - t_0)} x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{A(t - \tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

Setting $t_0 = k T_s$ and $t = (k+1) T_s$ and applying ZOH ($u(\tau) = u[k]$ for $kT_s \leq \tau < (k+1)T_s$):

$$
x[k+1] = e^{A T_s} x[k] + \left( \int_0^{T_s} e^{A \lambda} d\lambda \right) B u[k]
$$

Evaluating the integral using $\int_0^{T_s} e^{A\lambda} d\lambda = A^{-1}(e^{A T_s} - I)$:

$$
\boxed{\,x[k+1] = A_d x[k] + B_d u[k]\,}
$$

where:

$$
A_d = e^{A T_s}, \qquad B_d = A^{-1}(e^{A T_s} - I) B
$$

**Engineering Significance:**
- This form is what **every embedded controller algorithm** (PID, MPC, LQR) operates on.
- It bridges **physical-world continuity** with **cyber-world discreteness**.
- It is the foundation for **digital twin** simulation and **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Compiled from KTU Board Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Conflating CPS with IoT** — Students often write "CPS is just IoT with sensors." This loses **2–3 marks** immediately. The *closed-loop control* distinction is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Omitting Real-Time Constraints** — A CPS definition that does not mention real-time behavior is **incomplete**. Examiners specifically award 1 mark for this keyword.
> 3. **Skipping the Feedback Loop** — Always draw the **closed loop** in diagrams. Open-loop arrows signal a fundamental conceptual gap.
> 4. **Forgetting Units in Formulas** — $\tau_{cps}$ must be in **seconds (s)**, $f_s$ in **Hz**. Unit errors invite negative marking.
> 5. **Confusing Sensors with Actuators** — A sensor *measures*, an actuator *acts*. In block diagrams, draw both clearly and label them.
> 6. **Missing the "Cyber" half** — When asked for CPS examples, students list only "smart sensors" or "IoT devices" without the *cyber decision-making + actuation* component. Always include both halves of the loop.
> 7. **No application context in 14-mark answers** — A long answer must include **one full worked example** (e.g., autonomous vehicle, smart grid, insulin pump). A generic answer without an example loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CPS = Computation + Communication + Control** (the "3C" mantra).
- **Core enabler:** Closed-loop feedback between cyber and physical layers.
- **CPS ≠ IoT:** IoT observes; CPS *observes, decides, and physically acts* under real-time constraints.
- **Three layers:** Physical (plant), Cyber (processors, algorithms), Communication (sensors, actuators, networks).
- **Key real-time metric:** $\tau_{cps} = \tau_s + \tau_n + \tau_c + \tau_a \leq T_{deadline}$.
- **Hard real-time** = missing a deadline is a system failure (safety-critical CPS).
- **Sampling rule:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{bw}$ (Nyquist) — with engineering safety factor 5–10×.
- **Discrete state-space (ZOH):** $x[k+1] = e^{A T_s} x[k] + A^{-1}(e^{A T_s} - I) B u[k]$.
- **PID control law (discrete):** $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e[k] + K_d \Delta e[k]$.
- **CPS examples to memorize:** autonomous vehicles, smart grid, medical devices (pacemaker, insulin pump), industrial robots, UAVs, smart cities.
- **Standards:** ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (aerospace), IEC 61508 (industrial), IEC 62443 (cybersecurity).
- **Lifecycle:** Requirements → Modeling → Co-Design → Implementation → Integration → V&V → Deployment → Operations → Retirement (with feedback to requirements).
- **Always draw the closed loop** in any CPS diagram — it is the visual hallmark of CPS in exam scripts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPS Architecture and Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT865 - SYSTEMS ENGINEERING AND INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** CPS Architecture and Integration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (the *cyber* component) are tightly integrated with physical processes (the *physical* component) through sensors and actuators, where the embedded computation and physical dynamics continuously interact across spatial and temporal scales. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT865 - Module 4):** > CPS Architecture refers to the structured, layered organization of sensing, computation, communication, and actuation subsystems that together enable seamless bidirectional interaction between the digital and physical worlds. **Integration** in this context denotes the systematic unification of heterogeneous components into a coherent, mission-capable system-of-systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **modern electric car** (e.g., a Tesla Model 3): - The **physical layer** is the actual car — wheels, battery, brakes, motor, chassis. - The **cyber layer** is the on-board computer, the touchscreen, the AI chip, the navigation software. - The **sensors** (cameras, ultrasonic, LiDAR) continuously measure the physical world. - The **actuators** (steering motor, brake-by-wire, accelerator) physically alter the car's behavior. - The **integration** is the entire "feel" of the car responding instantly to your touch, autopilot braking when it "sees" a pedestrian, and the battery management system rebalancing cells in real time. In short: **CPS = Brain (Cyber) + Body (Physical) + Nerves (Sensors/Actuators)**, working as one unified organism. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Expected):** > - **Sampling Rate (f_s):** typically **1 Hz to 1 MHz** depending on application > - **Latency Bound (τ):** often **< 1 ms** for hard real-time CPS > - **Reliability Target:** **99.999% ("five nines")** for safety-critical CPS > - **Spatial Coupling:** cross-domain between **IT (bits/s)** and **PT (m/s, N·m, J)** ### 1.3 Why CPS Architecture Matters in Systems Engineering In classical systems engineering, software and hardware were developed largely *in silos*, then glued together. CPS demands **co-design**: the control algorithm, the communication bus, the physical plant, and the safety logic must be architected **simultaneously**, because failures cascade across the cyber-physical boundary. A poor architecture results in: - *Race conditions* between sensor reading and actuator command - *Timing violations* in safety-critical loops - *Brittle integration* where one vendor's subsystem breaks another's - *Unverifiable emergent behavior* > [!TIP] > A robust CPS architecture follows the **MAPE-K loop** (Monitor, Analyze, Plan, Execute, Knowledge) — a foundational autonomic-computing principle that KTU examiners love asking about. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 5-Layer Reference Architecture of CPS Modern CPS, as standardized in frameworks like the **NIST CPS Framework (2016, public domain)** and adopted in the KTU 2024 PECPT865 curriculum, follows a **5-layer architectural pattern**: | Layer # | Layer Name | Primary Function | Typical Technology | KTU Tag | |--------:|------------|------------------|--------------------|---------| | **L1** | **Physical Layer (PL)** | Real-world processes, energy, mechanics | Motors, valves, batteries, robotic arms | Body | | **L2** | **Sensing & Actuation Layer (S/A)** | Signal transduction, A/D & D/A conversion | MEMS sensors, ADC/DAC, PWM drivers | Nerves | | **L3** | **Communication Layer (CL)** | Data transport, time synchronization | CAN, EtherCAT, 5G, TSN, MQTT | Spinal Cord | | **L4** | **Computation Layer (CyL)** | Decision logic, control, ML inference | RTOS, embedded Linux, FPGAs, edge GPUs | Brain | | **L5** | **Application / Service Layer (AL)** | Mission logic, user interaction, analytics | Cloud microservices, HMI, digital twins | Mind | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Integration Principle:** Layers are *not* stacked in isolation — information and energy flow **bidirectionally**. Feedback from L4 to L1 (control command) and feedforward from L1 to L5 (telemetry) must be **architecturally explicit**. ### 2.2 Core CPS Integration Approaches There are three dominant integration strategies, each with different architectural implications: **Approach 1 — Tight Coupling (Cyber-Physical Co-Design)** - Control law and physical plant designed jointly. - Sub-millisecond latency. - Used in: aerospace fly-by-wire, surgical robots, ABS braking. **Approach 2 — Loose Coupling (Service-Oriented CPS)** - Subsystems communicate via messages, often over IP/Ethernet. - Latency: **10 ms to 1 s**. - Used in: smart grid, building automation, Industry 4.0 cells. **Approach 3 — Federated / System-of-Systems Integration** - Independently operable CPS nodes coordinated by a supervisory layer. - Latency: **seconds to minutes**. - Used in: smart cities, traffic management, multi-robot swarms. ### 2.3 The MAPE-K Control Loop (Foundation of Self-Adaptive CPS) The **MAPE-K loop** (from IBM's Autonomic Computing, 2003, public domain) is the canonical self-management architecture for CPS: - **M — Monitor:** Collect sensor data (temperature, position, voltage, network load). - **A — Analyze:** Compare current state vs. desired state; detect anomalies using rules or ML. - **P — Plan:** Generate a corrective action (e.g., re-route, throttle, switch controller). - **E — Execute:** Dispatch commands to actuators or virtual actuators (scaling, migration). - **K — Knowledge:** Shared data model accessed by all four phases. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units | Where Used | |---|-------------------|---------|---------------|------------| | 1 | $\tau = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{comm} + t_{actuate}$ | End-to-end CPS latency budget | seconds (s) | Real-time CPS design | | 2 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Minimum sensor sampling frequency | Hz | Sampling theorem application | | 3 | $J = \int_{0}^{\infty} e(t)^{2}\,dt$ (ISE) | Integral Square Error — control cost | dimensionless | Controller tuning | | 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (Reliability) | Probability of no failure up to time $t$ | dimensionless | Safety analysis | | 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate from Mean Time Between Failures | 1/h | MTBF-based design | | 6 | $QoS_{CPS} = f(\tau, R, P_{loss})$ | Composite Quality-of-Service metric | mixed | Networked CPS design | | 7 | $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t))$ (plant) | Continuous physical dynamics | state vector | Control-theoretic CPS | | 8 | $x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k]$ | Discrete cyber-side state update | sampled signal | Digital control in CPS | | 9 | $T_{deadline} \geq \tau$ | Schedulability condition (hard real-time) | seconds | RTOS task design | | 10 | $B = \dfrac{\# \text{correctly classified}}{\# \text{total samples}}$ | ML inference accuracy in CPS perception | dimensionless | ML-in-the-loop CPS | > [!TIP] > **Avoid the pipe trap:** In KTU answer sheets, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ for absolute value — never the raw $\vert$ inside a markdown table. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility CPS Architecture and Integration is foundational to: - **Automotive:** Autonomous Vehicle Stack (SAE Levels 0–5) - **Healthcare:** Closed-loop insulin pumps, robotic surgery (Da Vinci) - **Energy:** Smart grid with DER coordination - **Manufacturing:** Industrial IoT, digital twin-driven production - **Aerospace:** UAV swarm coordination, F-35 sensor fusion - **Civil Infrastructure:** Structural health monitoring of bridges and dams ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of End-to-End CPS Latency Bound We derive the **worst-case latency** for a single control loop in a tightly-coupled CPS, which is the most common KTU Part B 14-mark derivation. **Given:** - Sensor acquisition time: $t_{sense}$ - Analog-to-Digital conversion: $t_{ADC}$ - Communication propagation: $t_{comm}$ - Computation (control law) execution: $t_{compute}$ - Digital-to-Analog conversion: $t_{DAC}$ - Actuator response: $t_{actuate}$ **Step 1: Write total latency as sum of contributions** $$ \tau_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{ADC} + t_{comm} + t_{compute} + t_{DAC} + t_{actuate} $$ **Step 2: Apply the schedulability constraint (hard real-time CPS)** For a control task with period $T$ and deadline $D$, the system is schedulable **iff** (if and only if): $$ D \geq \tau_{total} $$ **Step 3: Apply response-time analysis for Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** For $n$ tasks with priorities $P_1 > P_2 > \ldots > P_n$, the worst-case response time of task $i$ is given by the smallest $R_i$ satisfying: $$ R_i = C_i + \sum_{j < i} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ where: - $C_i$ = worst-case execution time of task $i$ - $T_j$ = period of higher-priority task $j$ - The sum runs over all higher-priority tasks $j < i$ **Step 4: Solve iteratively** Initialize $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$. Then iterate: $$ R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j < i} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ The iteration converges when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$. **Step 5: Schedulability verdict** The system is schedulable if, for all $i$: $$ R_i \leq D_i $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Choice vs. Application | Application Domain | Coupling | Latency Budget | Reliability | Recommended Architecture | |--------------------|----------|----------------|-------------|--------------------------| | Surgical robot | Tight | $\tau < 1$ ms | $R > 0.999999$ | Tightly-coupled, custom SoC | | Autonomous vehicle perception | Tight | $\tau < 50$ ms | $R > 0.99999$ | Tight (sensor-fusion ECU) | | Smart thermostat | Loose | $\tau < 5$ s | $R > 0.99$ | Loose, MQTT over Wi-Fi | | Wind farm coordination | Federated | $\tau < 60$ s | $R > 0.999$ | Federated, SCADA backbone | | UAV swarm search-and-rescue | Federated | $\tau < 500$ ms | $R > 0.9999$ | Federated + edge mesh | | Smart grid load balancing | Federated | $\tau < 1$ min | $R > 0.9999$ | Federated, IEC 61850 | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A CPS has three periodic tasks on a Rate-Monotonic scheduler. | Task | Execution $C_i$ (ms) | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Deadline $D_i$ (ms) | |------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------| | T1 (highest) | 1 | 4 | 4 | | T2 | 2 | 6 | 6 | | T3 (lowest) | 3 | 10 | 10 | **Step 1: Check T1** $R_1 = C_1 = 1$ ms. Since $1 \leq 4$, T1 is schedulable. **Step 2: Check T2** $R_2^{(0)} = 2$ First iteration: $$ R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ Second iteration: $$ R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ Converged: $R_2 = 3 \leq 6$. ✓ T2 schedulable. **Step 3: Check T3** $R_3^{(0)} = 3$ Iteration 1: $$ R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 $$ Iteration 2: $$ R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 $$ Iteration 3: $$ R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9 $$ Iteration 4: $$ R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 $$ Iteration 5: $$ R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 $$ Converged: $R_3 = 10$ ms. Since $10 \leq 10$, T3 is **just barely schedulable**. ✓ **Step 4: Total CPU utilization check (Liu & Layland bound)** $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} = \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{2}{6} + \dfrac{3}{10} $$ $$ U = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.30 = 0.883 $$ Liu & Layland bound for $n=3$: $U_{bound} = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.779$. Since $0.883 > 0.779$, the utilization bound is **violated**, but the exact response-time analysis still shows the system is schedulable — a classic KTU trap question. > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** Liu & Layland bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. A system can exceed the bound and still be schedulable, as demonstrated above. KTU examiners frequently test this subtlety. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CPS 5-Layer Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB APP["L5 APPLICATION AND SERVICE LAYER
Mission Logic, HMI, Digital Twin, Cloud Analytics"] CMP["L4 COMPUTATION LAYER
Control Laws, RTOS, Edge ML, FPGA Inference"] COMM["L3 COMMUNICATION LAYER
CAN, EtherCAT, TSN, 5G, MQTT, OPC UA"] SACT["L2 SENSING AND ACTUATION LAYER
MEMS, ADC, DAC, PWM, Signal Conditioning"] PHY["L1 PHYSICAL LAYER
Motors, Valves, Battery, Robotic Arm, Plant Dynamics"] APP -->|Service Request| CMP CMP -->|Telemetry Up| APP CMP -->|Control Command Down| COMM COMM -->|Sensor Data Up| CMP COMM -->|Actuator Setpoint| SACT SACT -->|Raw Signal| PHY PHY -->|Measurement| SACT SACT -->|Feedback| COMM ``` ### 4.2 MAPE-K Self-Adaptive Loop in CPS ```mermaid flowchart LR MON["M MONITOR
Sensor Polling
Telemetry Aggregation"] ANA["A ANALYZE
Anomaly Detection
State Estimation"] PLN["P PLAN
Reconfiguration Policy
Control Synthesis"] EXE["E EXECUTE
Actuator Dispatch
Effectors Action"] KNO["K KNOWLEDGE BASE
Shared World Model
Policies, History, Topology"] MON -->|Raw Observations| ANA ANA -->|Diagnosed State| PLN PLN -->|Action Plan| EXE EXE -->|Effect| MON KNO -.->|Read Write| MON KNO -.->|Read Write| ANA KNO -.->|Read Write| PLN KNO -.->|Read Write| EXE ``` ### 4.3 Integration Strategy Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START["New CPS Integration Requirement"] Q1{"Latency Budget
Less Than 10 ms?"} Q2{"Subsystems Owned by
Single Organization?"} Q3{"Real-Time Safety
Critical?"} TIGHT["TIGHT COUPLING
Co-Design, Custom SoC
RTOS, Hard Real-Time"] LOOSE["LOOSE COUPLING
Service Bus, IP Network
Soft Real-Time"] FED["FEDERATED SOS
Supervisory Coordinator
Loose Time Coupling"] START --> Q1 Q1 -->|Yes| Q3 Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q3 -->|Yes| TIGHT Q3 -->|No| LOOSE Q2 -->|Yes| LOOSE Q2 -->|No| FED ``` ### 4.4 CPS Integration Viewpoint (System-of-Systems) ```mermaid flowchart TB SOS["CPS SYSTEM OF SYSTEMS
Smart City Transportation"] subgraph SUB1["Subsystem A Traffic CPS"] A1["Roadside Sensors"] A2["Signal Controller"] A1 --> A2 end subgraph SUB2["Subsystem B Vehicle CPS"] B1["Onboard ECU"] B2["V2X Radio"] B1 --> B2 end subgraph SUB3["Subsystem C Cloud CPS"] C1["Traffic Predictor"] C2["Route Optimizer"] C1 --> C2 end COORD["SUPERVISORY COORDINATOR
City Operations Center"] SUB1 --> COORD SUB2 --> COORD SUB3 --> COORD SOS -.-> COORD ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading these diagrams for KTU exams:** Notice how each Mermaid block uses *purely alphanumeric* node IDs (`MON`, `ANA`, `PLN`, `EXE`, `KNO`, etc.) and *clean uppercase text labels* — this avoids the rendering bugs that frequently break student-submitted diagrams during viva or assignment review. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT865]** *Define a Cyber-Physical System (CPS). List any four real-world examples.* **Model Answer:** A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system that tightly integrates computation, networking, and physical processes, where embedded computers monitor and control the physical processes, usually through feedback loops where physical processes affect computations and vice versa. **Four real-world examples (any four, ½ mark each):** 1. Autonomous vehicles (self-driving cars) 2. Medical devices such as closed-loop insulin pumps and robotic surgery systems 3. Smart grid and energy management systems 4. Industrial robots and collaborative robots (cobots) on a smart factory floor 5. Aerospace fly-by-wire and UAV swarms 6. Smart home automation (e.g., Nest thermostat) *(½ mark for clean definition + 2 marks for examples)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECPT865]** *What is the MAPE-K loop? Name its five phases in the correct order.* **Model Answer:** The **MAPE-K loop** is the canonical reference architecture for self-managing, autonomic CPS, originally proposed in IBM's Autonomic Computing initiative (public domain reference, 2003). The five phases, in their correct operational order, are: 1. **M — Monitor:** Collect sensor data and system metrics. 2. **A — Analyze:** Compare observed state against desired state; detect anomalies. 3. **P — Plan:** Synthesize a corrective or optimization action. 4. **E — Execute:** Dispatch the action to actuators or virtual actuators. 5. **K — Knowledge:** A shared data repository (world model, policies, history) that all four control phases read from and write to. *(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for correctly ordered phases — KTU examiners penalize reordering.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation 2014 (amended 2024)**, every Module-based Part B question must offer an **internal choice**. Below, Question A and Question B are **two fully independent alternatives**. --- #### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT865, Module 4]** **A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer reference architecture** of a Cyber-Physical System. Clearly state the role of each layer and the bidirectional information flow between them. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The five-layer CPS architecture, accepted as the de-facto reference model in the KTU 2024 syllabus, is described from top to bottom as follows: **Layer 5 — Application / Service Layer:** Houses the mission logic, human-machine interface (HMI), digital twin, and cloud-side analytics. It defines *what* the system must accomplish from the user's perspective (e.g., "keep room at 22 °C", "deliver parcel in 30 min"). **Layer 4 — Computation Layer:** Implements the control laws, scheduling decisions, and on-device machine-learning inference. Runs on RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks) or embedded Linux. This is the *brain* that converts sensor data into actuator commands. **Layer 3 — Communication Layer:** Provides deterministic, time-synchronized transport. Common protocols include CAN and CAN-FD (automotive), EtherCAT and TSN (industrial), 5G URLLC (cellular), and MQTT/OPC UA (IoT). Guarantees bounded latency and minimal jitter. **Layer 2 — Sensing & Actuation Layer:** Contains transducers (temperature, pressure, IMU, LiDAR), signal conditioning circuits, ADC/DAC converters, and PWM or current drivers that bridge the analog physical world and the digital cyber world. **Layer 1 — Physical Layer:** The actual engineered physical process — motors, pumps, batteries, robotic arms, vehicle chassis, chemical reactor. Governed by continuous-time dynamics such as $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t))$. **Bidirectional Flow (Diagram Description — 2 marks for the diagram):** - **Upward (telemetry):** Physical → Sensor → Communication → Computation → Application - **Downward (command):** Application → Computation → Communication → Actuator → Physical The two flows close a feedback loop. The architecture's value is the **explicit separation of concerns**: each layer can be designed, tested, and replaced independently, which is a cornerstone of the modular systems-engineering approach. --- **A. (b)** Differentiate between **tight coupling**, **loose coupling**, and **federated integration** in CPS. For each, state one suitable application and justify the choice based on latency and reliability requirements. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Dimension | **Tight Coupling** | **Loose Coupling** | **Federated Integration** | |-----------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------------------| | **Definition** | Cyber and physical designed jointly; sub-millisecond feedback | Subsystems interact via messages over a network | Independent CPS coordinated by a supervisory layer | | **Latency** | $\tau < 1$ ms | $\tau = 10$ ms to $1$ s | $\tau =$ seconds to minutes | | **Reliability** | $R > 0.99999$ | $R > 0.999$ | $R > 0.99$ | | **Standard** | Custom SoC, RTOS | MQTT, OPC UA, REST | SCADA, IEC 61850, HLA | | **Failure Impact** | Catastrophic if violated | Graceful degradation | Mission abort, no casualties | **Suitable Applications (1 each, 2 marks):** 1. **Tight Coupling → Robotic surgical arm (Da Vinci):** Patient safety demands $\tau < 1$ ms and $R > 0.999999$; any missed deadline could cause tissue damage. Justified. 2. **Loose Coupling → Building HVAC automation:** Comfort, not safety-critical; $\tau < 5$ s is acceptable; cheap Wi-Fi/MQTT suffices. Justified. 3. **Federated → Smart city traffic management:** Hundreds of intersections and vehicles operate semi-independently; a city-wide coordinator balances flows every 1–5 min. Justified. **Conclusion:** The tighter the safety-criticality, the tighter the coupling. Federated integration is the *only* economically viable option when CPS subsystems belong to different organizations (different vendors, different administrative domains). --- #### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Question A)* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECPT865, Module 4]** **B. (a)** Explain the **MAPE-K reference model** for self-adaptive CPS. Illustrate with a concrete example of an autonomous vehicle's adaptive cruise control (ACC) subsystem, showing what data flows through each phase. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **MAPE-K loop** is the standard architectural pattern for building self-managing, autonomic Cyber-Physical Systems. It consists of four control phases — Monitor, Analyze, Plan, Execute — all sharing a common Knowledge base. **Concrete Example — Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** A vehicle traveling on a highway uses radar and camera to maintain a safe gap from the car ahead. The MAPE-K phases operate as follows: 1. **Monitor (M):** Radar reports distance to lead vehicle $d(t)$ every $20$ ms. Camera classifies lead vehicle type. Own vehicle speed $v_{own}$ and throttle/brake positions are also sampled. 2. **Analyze (A):** Compare measured gap $d(t)$ against desired gap $d_{des} = \tau_{gap} \cdot v_{own}$, where $\tau_{gap} = 1.5$ s. Compute error $e(t) = d_{des} - d(t)$. Detect anomalies (e.g., sudden cut-in). 3. **Plan (P):** A Model Predictive Control (MPC) solver computes the optimal acceleration $a^{*}(t)$ over a $2$-second prediction horizon, subject to safety constraints $a \in [-3, 1.5] \text{ m/s}^2$. 4. **Execute (E):** Dispatch $a^{*}(t)$ to the drive-by-wire ECU. If $a^{*} < 0$, the brake actuator is engaged; if $a^{*} > 0$, the throttle actuator. 5. **Knowledge (K):** Stores the digital twin of nearby vehicles, learned driver preferences, road curvature map, and historical traffic patterns. All four phases read/write to K. **Flow of Data Through MAPE-K (3 marks for the example flow):** $$ \text{Sensors} \xrightarrow{M} \text{Measurements} \xrightarrow{A} \text{Error} \xrightarrow{P} \text{Action} \xrightarrow{E} \text{Actuators} $$ $$ \text{K} \longleftrightarrow \text{M, A, P, E} \quad \text{(shared world model)} $$ **Significance:** Without MAPE-K, the ACC would be a *static* controller. With MAPE-K, the same CPS can adapt to weather (via K), driver mood (via K), and traffic (via M→A). --- **B. (b)** A CPS has three Rate-Monotonic tasks with the following parameters: $C_1 = 1$ ms, $T_1 = 4$ ms; $C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 6$ ms; $C_3 = 3$ ms, $T_3 = 10$ ms. Using response-time analysis, determine if the system is schedulable. Also compute the total CPU utilization and compare with the Liu–Layland bound for $n=3$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** *(Full step-by-step RTA was demonstrated in §3.3 of these notes. The compact answer key follows.)* **Step 1 — Response-Time Analysis:** $R_1 = 1$ ms. Since $1 \leq 4$ → T1 schedulable. $R_2 = 2 + \lceil 2/4 \rceil \cdot 1 = 3$ ms. Since $3 \leq 6$ → T2 schedulable. $R_3$ iteration: - $R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$ - $R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 6/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$ - $R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \lceil 7/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 7/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$ - $R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \lceil 9/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$ - $R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \lceil 10/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 10/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$ (converged) $R_3 = 10$ ms. Since $10 \leq 10$ → T3 just schedulable. **Verdict:** The system is **schedulable** (just barely) under Rate-Monotonic Scheduling. **Step 2 — CPU Utilization:** $$ U = \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{2}{6} + \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883 = 88.3\% $$ **Step 3 — Liu–Layland Bound for $n=3$:** $$ U_{LL} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7797 \approx 77.97\% $$ **Step 4 — Comparison:** Since $U = 88.3\% > U_{LL} = 78.0\%$, the Liu–Layland bound is **violated**. However, the **exact** response-time analysis shows the system is still schedulable. This proves the Liu–Layland bound is a sufficient, not necessary, condition — a classic KTU trap. **Marks distribution:** 1 mark per task RTA + 1 mark utilization + 1 mark LL bound + 1 mark comparison + 1 mark final verdict. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students lose marks on CPS Architecture questions:** > > 1. **Confusing CPS with IoT.** IoT is a *subset* of the Communication Layer of CPS. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for equating them. > 2. **Drawing the 5-layer diagram as unidirectional.** A CPS architecture is *bidirectional* — telemetry up, command down. Unidirectional arrows = 0 marks for the diagram. > 3. **Forgetting the K in MAPE-K.** Students often write only M-A-P-E. The Knowledge base is what makes the loop *autonomic*; omitting K costs 1 mark. > 4. **Using Liu–Layland bound as a hard schedulability test.** It is *sufficient but not necessary*. The exact response-time analysis is the only definitive test. > 5. **Mixing up the Nyquist criterion with Shannon.** In KTU board style, write: "Sampling frequency must be **strictly greater than** twice the maximum signal frequency" — not "at least". Examiners are pedantic about strict vs. non-strict inequalities. > 6. **Vague application examples.** Saying "cars" is worth 0 marks. Saying "Toyota Prius hybrid control with CAN bus at 500 kbps" is worth full marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-density revision checklist for CPS Architecture & Integration (Module 4):** - ✅ **CPS** = tight integration of computation, communication, and physical processes via sensors/actuators and feedback loops. - ✅ **5-Layer Architecture:** Physical → Sensing/Actuation → Communication → Computation → Application (bidirectional). - ✅ **Three Integration Strategies:** Tight (sub-ms, safety-critical), Loose (10 ms–1 s, IoT), Federated (SoS, multi-org). - ✅ **MAPE-K Loop:** Monitor → Analyze → Plan → Execute, with shared **K**nowledge base. Foundation of self-adaptive CPS. - ✅ **End-to-end latency:** $\tau = t_{sense} + t_{ADC} + t_{comm} + t_{compute} + t_{DAC} + t_{actuate}$; must satisfy $\tau \leq D$ (deadline). - ✅ **Nyquist Sampling:** $f_s > 2 f_{max}$ (strict inequality for KTU). - ✅ **Liu–Layland bound:** $U_{LL} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — sufficient but **not** necessary for RMS schedulability. - ✅ **Exact RTA iteration:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j < i} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ until convergence. - ✅ **Reliability metric:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ with $\lambda = 1 / MTBF$. - ✅ **Standard protocols to remember for KTU:** CAN (automotive), EtherCAT/TSN (industrial), MQTT/OPC UA (IoT), 5G URLLC (cellular CPS), IEC 61850 (grid). - ✅ **Reference standards (public domain, cite safely in answers):** NIST CPS Framework (2016), IBM Autonomic Computing MAPE-K (2003), ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010 (architecture description), SAE J3016 (driving automation levels). - ✅ **Common KTU comparison pairs to know:** - CPS vs. IoT (CPS ⊃ IoT) - CPS vs. Embedded System (CPS has explicit feedback, networked, multi-domain) - CPS vs. Digital Twin (DT is a virtual replica; CPS includes it as one layer) - Tight vs. Loose Coupling (latency, reliability, design ownership) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-line exam strategy for PECPT865 Module 4:** > In every 14-mark question, always **draw the block diagram first** (2 marks secured), then **define each layer in one line** (3 marks), then **state the latency/reliability implication** (2 marks), then **give one real-world numerical example** (2 marks), then close with a 2-3 line **system-engineering trade-off discussion** (5 marks). This structure maps perfectly to the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

topics.title: Security and Privacy in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT865 - SYSTEMS ENGINEERING AND INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security and Privacy in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cyber-Physical System (CPS)?

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered computational and physical system whose elements are deeply intertwined through networked communication, sensors, actuators, and control loops, where the cyber (computation, communication, monitoring) and physical (sensing, actuation, processes) components co-operate in real-time to achieve a unified functionality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with NSF/Rajkumar et al.):**
> A CPS is "a physical and engineered system whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a computing and communication core." It tightly couples computation, networking, and physical processes.

---

## 1.2 Why Security and Privacy are Critical in CPS

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Unlike traditional IT systems where a security breach leaks *data*, a CPS breach can leak *data **and** cause physical harm* — to humans, infrastructure, or the environment.

In conventional Information Technology (IT) systems, the worst-case impact of a cyberattack is data theft, financial loss, or denial of service. In CPS, the attack surface extends into the **physical world**:

| CPS Domain | Physical Consequence of Cyber Attack |
|---|---|
| Smart Grid | Blackouts, equipment destruction, cascading failures |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Collisions, loss of life |
| Industrial Control (SCADA/ICS) | Pipeline rupture, chemical release |
| Medical Devices (IoMT) | Fatal drug dosage, device malfunction |
| Smart Buildings | Lockouts, fire alarm suppression |

This is why **Security and Privacy** is a non-negotiable Module 4 topic in Systems Engineering & Integration under the KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Modern Hospital Patient Monitor**
>
> Imagine a hospital patient connected to a heart monitor (CPS). The *sensor* on the chest is the physical interface; the *display and algorithm* is the cyber interface; the *alarm and IV drip controller* are the actuators.
>
> - **Security failure** = A hacker spoofs the heart-rate reading → the monitor reads "Normal" while the patient is in cardiac arrest → no alarm fires → patient dies.
> - **Privacy failure** = The patient's medical telemetry is intercepted → identity and health records are exposed → discrimination by insurers.
>
> This is exactly why Security and Privacy in CPS is fundamentally different from classical IT security — a single spoofed sensor reading can become a **life-safety violation**.

---

## 1.4 Foundational Triad: Safety, Security, Privacy in CPS

KTU 2024 emphasizes three pillars that must be engineered simultaneously:

### (a) Safety
The ability of the CPS to operate without causing unacceptable risk of physical harm to people, property, or the environment, **even in the presence of faults or adversarial actions**.

### (b) Security
The protection of CPS assets (data, hardware, software, communication) against unauthorized access, modification, destruction, or disclosure — preserving **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)**.

### (c) Privacy
The right of individuals and organizations to control how their data — collected, transmitted, and processed by CPS — is gathered, stored, used, and shared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU high-yield):**
> **Safety ≠ Security.** A system can be *safe* under random faults but *unsafe* under deliberate attack. Both must be designed in.

---

## 1.5 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Vocabulary)

**Confidentiality** — Assurance that information is disclosed only to authorized entities.

**Integrity** — Assurance that data, commands, or physical state variables are not modified in an unauthorized or undetected manner.

**Availability** — Assurance that the CPS services (sensing, control, actuation) are accessible when needed by legitimate users and controllers.

**Authenticity** — Assurance that the source of a message or command is genuine and verifiable.

**Non-repudiation** — Guarantee that a sender cannot deny having sent a control command or measurement.

**Accountability** — Ability to trace actions in the CPS to a specific entity, device, or firmware version.

---

## 1.6 KTU Board-Favorite Highlight Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favorites (Module 4 — Security & Privacy):**
> 1. Difference between IT Security and CPS Security.
> 2. CIA Triad + Safety + Privacy extension (called the "P-CIA" extension).
> 3. Attack Surfaces in CPS (sensor, actuator, network, controller, firmware).
> 4. Famous CPS attacks: **Stuxnet, Mirai Botnet, Ukraine Power Grid (BlackEnergy), Jeep Cherokee Hack**.
> 5. Defense-in-Depth and Security-by-Design.
> 6. Privacy-preserving mechanisms: anonymization, differential privacy, homomorphic encryption.
> 7. Risk Management frameworks: NIST CSF, ISO/IEC 27001, IEC 62443.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Extended CIA+SP Triad (P-CIA Framework)

Classical IT security rests on **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability**. For CPS, KTU 2024 mandates extension to include **Safety, Privacy, and Authenticity** because of the physical consequences.

$$
\text{CPS Security Properties} = \{C, I, A, S, P, Au, NR\}
$$

where:
- $C$ = Confidentiality
- $I$ = Integrity
- $A$ = Availability
- $S$ = Safety
- $P$ = Privacy
- $Au$ = Authenticity
- $NR$ = Non-Repudiation

---

## 2.2 Threat Modeling in CPS

A **threat model** systematically identifies what assets must be protected, who the adversaries are, what attack vectors they use, and what defenses are appropriate.

### 2.2.1 STRIDE Threat Classification (Adapted for CPS)

| STRIDE Category | IT Equivalent | CPS-Specific Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Forged user ID | Forged sensor reading, spoofed GPS |
| **T**ampering | DB modification | Modified control law, altered actuator set-point |
| **R**epudiation | Denied transaction | Replayed control command denied |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Data leak | Patient telemetry, smart-meter usage exposed |
| **D**enial of Service | Server down | Control loop delayed → instability |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Root access | PLC reprogrammed by attacker |

---

## 2.3 Attack Surfaces in CPS (KTU High-Yield Diagram Topic)

A CPS attack surface is the sum of all points where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter, extract data, or manipulate the system.

### The Four Major Attack Surfaces

1. **Sensor Attack Surface**
   - Physical tampering, sensor spoofing, GPS jamming, false data injection.
2. **Actuator Attack Surface**
   - Command injection, replay attacks, denial-of-actuation.
3. **Network/Communication Attack Surface**
   - MITM (Man-in-the-Middle), eavesdropping, DoS, replay.
4. **Controller/Firmware Attack Surface**
   - Malware (Stuxnet), rootkits, supply-chain compromise, logic bomb.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defense-in-Depth Principle (KTU Board Favorite):**
> No single control is sufficient. CPS security requires layered defenses across all four attack surfaces.

---

## 2.4 CPS Attack Models — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.4.1 Sensor Attack (False Data Injection — FDI)

**Goal:** Compromise system state estimation.

**Steps:**
1. Attacker identifies sensor(s) $s_i$.
2. Replaces true measurement $y_i = h_i(x) + v_i$ with malicious reading $y_i^a = h_i(x) + v_i + \delta_i$.
3. State estimator produces biased estimate $\hat{x}^a \neq \hat{x}$.
4. Controller acts on biased estimate → unsafe physical behavior.

### 2.4.2 Actuator Attack (Command Injection)

**Steps:**
1. Attacker gains access to the control channel.
2. Injects false command $u^a$ replacing $u_{\text{true}}$.
3. Physical process $x_{k+1} = f(x_k, u^a)$ diverges from set-point.

### 2.4.3 Replay Attack

1. Attacker records legitimate traffic during normal operation.
2. Replays captured commands during attack window.
3. Detector sees "valid" traffic but operation is unsafe because context has changed.

### 2.4.4 Time-Delay / DoS Attack on Control Loop

1. Attacker jams wireless link between sensor and controller.
2. Controller operates on stale measurement.
3. Control loop becomes unstable — equivalent to destabilizing a feedback system.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Board-Tested for Module 4 derivations.**

### Table: Quantitative CPS Security Metrics

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk | $R = P \times I \times V$ | Probability $\times$ Impact $\times$ Vulnerability | dimensionless score |
| Attack Probability | $P(A) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Poisson process of attack attempts per unit time | probability |
| Mean-Time-to-Compromise (MTTC) | $\text{MTTC} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f_{\text{comp}}(t)\, dt$ | Expected time for successful breach | seconds / hours |
| Bayesian Detection Probability | $P(D \mid H) = \dfrac{P(H \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(H)}$ | Probability attack detected given hypothesis | dimensionless |
| Shannon Entropy (information leak) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Information content per symbol | bits |
| Differential Privacy Budget | $\varepsilon = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \varepsilon_t$ | Cumulative privacy loss across queries | dimensionless |
| Kullback–Leibler Divergence (anomaly) | $D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum_i P(i) \log_2 \dfrac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | Distance between normal vs. attack distribution | bits |
| Lyapunov Stability Under Attack | $\dot{V}(x) \leq -\alpha \vert x \vert^2 + \gamma \vert a \vert$ | Stability with bounded attack $a$ | — |
| Control Loop Stability Margin | $\phi_m = \angle G(j\omega_c) - (-\pi)$ | Phase margin under attack-induced delay | radians / degrees |
| Encryption Strength | $\mathcal{S} = 2^{n}$ | Strength of $n$-bit key | operations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above are written as `\vert`. For exam answers, students **must** write `\mid` or `\vert`, never `\vert` inside a markdown table cell that is itself delimited by `|`.

---

## 2.6 Risk Assessment Methodology (NIST CSF Mapping)

$$
\text{Risk Level} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact}, \text{Likelihood})
$$

The standard CPS risk equation used in KTU 2024 evaluation:

$$
R = T \times V \times I \times L
$$

where:
- $T$ = Threat score (0–10)
- $V$ = Vulnerability score (0–10)
- $I$ = Impact score (0–10)
- $L$ = Likelihood score (0–10)

**Decision Matrix:**

| Risk Score $R$ | Classification | Required Action |
|---|---|---|
| $0 - 100$ | Low | Accept |
| $101 - 400$ | Moderate | Mitigate |
| $401 - 800$ | High | Mitigate urgently |
| $801 - 10000$ | Critical | Avoid / Re-engineer |

---

## 2.7 Privacy Engineering Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Topic:** Students must know **at least three** privacy-preserving techniques.

| Mechanism | Principle | Use in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| Anonymization | Strip identifiers | Smart-meter data |
| Pseudonymization | Replace IDs with tokens | Connected vehicles (V2X) |
| Differential Privacy | Add calibrated noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Aggregate health telemetry |
| Homomorphic Encryption | Compute on ciphertext | Cloud-based SCADA analytics |
| Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC) | Joint computation without sharing raw data | Grid optimization |
| Federated Learning | Local training, shared model | Distributed CPS AI |
| Zero-Knowledge Proofs | Prove property without revealing data | Authentication of meter readings |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| CPS Domain | Security Mechanism Applied | Standards |
|---|---|---|
| Smart Grid | IEC 62351, NERC CIP | Substation encryption, key management |
| Industrial Control | IEC 62443 (ISA-99) | Zones & conduits, SL levels |
| Automotive | ISO/SAE 21434 | UNECE WP.29 cybersecurity regulation |
| Medical IoT | FDA Pre-Market Cybersecurity Guidance | Anonymized patient telemetry |
| IoT Devices | NIST IR 8259 | Baseline capabilities |
| General IT + CPS | NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) | Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section demonstrates the **complete derivation** of three KTU 2024 high-weight problems: (i) FDI Attack Impact on State Estimator, (ii) Risk Score Computation, and (iii) Differential Privacy Budget Consumption.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: False Data Injection (FDI) Impact on State Estimator

### Problem Setup

A linearized CPS measurement model:

$$
y = H x + e
$$

where:
- $y \in \mathbb{R}^m$ = sensor measurement vector
- $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ = true system state
- $H \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ = measurement Jacobian
- $e \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ = Gaussian noise with covariance $R$

The standard (un-attacked) **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** state estimate:

$$
\hat{x}_{\text{WLS}} = \arg\min_x \ (y - Hx)^{\top} R^{-1} (y - Hx)
$$

### Step 1 — Derivation of the Un-attacked Estimator

We differentiate the cost $J(x) = (y - Hx)^{\top} R^{-1} (y - Hx)$ w.r.t. $x$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -2 H^{\top} R^{-1} (y - Hx) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
H^{\top} R^{-1} y = H^{\top} R^{-1} H x
$$

Multiplying both sides by $(H^{\top} R^{-1} H)^{-1}$:

$$
\hat{x}_{\text{WLS}} = (H^{\top} R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^{\top} R^{-1} y
$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the Attack Vector

The attacker replaces $y$ with $y^a = y + a$, where $a \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the attack injection vector. The (naïve) estimator produces:

$$
\hat{x}^a = (H^{\top} R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^{\top} R^{-1} (y + a)
$$

Expanding:

$$
\hat{x}^a = \hat{x}_{\text{WLS}} + (H^{\top} R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^{\top} R^{-1} a
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Attack Impact on State

Let the **state estimation bias** be:

$$
\Delta x \;=\; \hat{x}^a - \hat{x}_{\text{WLS}} \;=\; \mathcal{M} \cdot a
$$

where the estimator sensitivity matrix is:

$$
\mathcal{M} = (H^{\top} R^{-1} H)^{-1} H^{\top} R^{-1}
$$

### Step 4 — Bad-Data Detector Check

A standard $\chi^2$-detector checks residual $r = y - H \hat{x}$. Under un-attacked conditions:

$$
\|r\|^2_{R^{-1}} = (y - H\hat{x})^{\top} R^{-1} (y - H\hat{x}) \sim \chi^2_m
$$

Under attack, the attacker can choose $a = H c$ for some $c \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (Liu, Reiter & Ning, 2009 — but KTU asks only the form, not citation). Then:

$$
r^a = y^a - H \hat{x}^a = (y + Hc) - H(\hat{x} + c) = y - H\hat{x} = r
$$

**Conclusion:** Detector sees identical residual → attack passes undetected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Student must derive both the bias equation $\Delta x = \mathcal{M} a$ and the undetectable condition $a = H c$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: KTU Risk Score Computation

### Problem Setup

Given four CPS assets with Threat, Vulnerability, Impact, and Likelihood scores (each 0–10):

| Asset | $T$ | $V$ | $I$ | $L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Substation PLC | 8 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Smart Meter | 5 | 8 | 3 | 9 |
| SCADA Server | 9 | 6 | 10 | 5 |
| Wireless Sensor | 6 | 9 | 4 | 8 |

### Step 1 — Apply the KTU Risk Equation

$$
R_i = T_i \times V_i \times I_i \times L_i
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Asset's Risk

**Asset 1: Substation PLC**

$$
R_1 = 8 \times 7 \times 9 \times 6 = 3024
$$

**Asset 2: Smart Meter**

$$
R_2 = 5 \times 8 \times 3 \times 9 = 1080
$$

**Asset 3: SCADA Server**

$$
R_3 = 9 \times 6 \times 10 \times 5 = 2700
$$

**Asset 4: Wireless Sensor**

$$
R_4 = 6 \times 9 \times 4 \times 8 = 1728
$$

### Step 3 — Classification Using Decision Matrix

| Asset | Risk $R$ | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Substation PLC | $3024$ | Critical |
| Smart Meter | $1080$ | Critical |
| SCADA Server | $2700$ | Critical |
| Wireless Sensor | $1728$ | Critical |

> All four assets require **immediate mitigation** under KTU's NIST-aligned policy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Differential Privacy Budget Consumption

### Problem Setup

A CPS smart-meter system releases aggregated energy consumption via an analytics API. Each query $Q_t$ consumes a privacy budget $\varepsilon_t$. The **total privacy loss** is:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \varepsilon_t
$$

### Step 1 — Add Calibrated Laplace Noise

For query $Q(x)$ with sensitivity $\Delta f$, the $\varepsilon$-DP mechanism returns:

$$
\mathcal{M}(x) = Q(x) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Compose Five Queries

Suppose queries $Q_1, \dots, Q_5$ have sensitivities $\Delta f_i = 1$ and budgets $\varepsilon_i = 0.2$ each.

Sequential composition:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} 0.2 = 1.0
$$

### Step 3 — Parallel Composition (Independent Subsets)

If the 5 queries are over **disjoint** subsets of meters, parallel composition applies:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max_i \varepsilon_i = 0.2
$$

This is why **partitioning data** across queries gives much stronger privacy — a critical KTU 2024 high-yield concept.

### Step 4 — Trade-off with Utility

Mean Squared Error of Laplace mechanism:

$$
\text{MSE}(\mathcal{M}) = \frac{2 (\Delta f)^2}{\varepsilon^2}
$$

For $\Delta f = 1, \varepsilon = 0.2$:

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{2 \times 1^2}{(0.2)^2} = \frac{2}{0.04} = 50
$$

This illustrates the **privacy–utility trade-off**: lower $\varepsilon$ ⇒ stronger privacy, but lower accuracy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Attack Type vs. Countermeasure

| Attack Type | Cyber Layer Affected | Physical Layer Affected | Best Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| False Data Injection (FDI) | Estimation algorithm | Sensor reading | Robust estimator, moving-horizon |
| Replay Attack | Authentication protocol | Actuator command | Time-stamp + nonce |
| DoS / Delay Attack | Network availability | Control loop stability | Redundant links, jitter buffers |
| Sensor Tampering | Hardware/physical access | Direct measurement | Tamper-evident seals, side-channel checks |
| Firmware Implant | Supply chain | Indirect — full system | Code signing, secure boot, TPM |
| GPS Spoofing | Wireless receiver | Position estimate | Multi-source fusion (IMU + GPS + vision) |
| Ransomware on SCADA | Operator HMI | Plant downtime | Air-gapped backups, MFA |
| Insider Attack | Logical access | Targeted sabotage | Audit logs, least privilege, separation of duties |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-4 Requirement:** Every Board answer should include at least one labelled schematic. Below are four examiner-grade Mermaid diagrams using strict alphanumeric node identifiers.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CPS Security Architecture (Defense-in-Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef phy fill:#FDE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#000
    classDef cyb fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#000
    classDef sec fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#000
    classDef pol fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#A16207,color:#000

    nodeA[Physical Process Plant]:::phy
    nodeB[Sensors and Actuators]:::phy
    nodeC[Field level RTU and PLC]:::cyb
    nodeD[Edge Gateway and Firewall]:::sec
    nodeE[Control Network DMZ]:::sec
    nodeF[SCADA HMI and Historian]:::cyb
    nodeG[Cloud Analytics and AI]:::cyb
    nodeH[Identity and Access Mgmt]:::sec
    nodeI[Intrusion Detection System]:::sec
    nodeJ[Key Management PKI]:::sec
    nodeK[Safety Instrumented System]:::sec
    nodeL[Audit Log and SIEM]:::sec
    nodeM[Governance Risk Compliance]:::pol

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeH -.-> nodeF
    nodeH -.-> nodeG
    nodeI -.-> nodeE
    nodeI -.-> nodeF
    nodeJ -.-> nodeD
    nodeJ -.-> nodeE
    nodeK -.-> nodeA
    nodeL -.-> nodeF
    nodeL -.-> nodeG
    nodeM -.-> nodeH
    nodeM -.-> nodeI
    nodeM -.-> nodeL
```

**Interpretation:** Defense-in-Depth layers — physical sensing/actuation, control, network segmentation, monitoring, governance — all with cross-cutting security services (IAM, IDS, PKI, SIS, SIEM, GRC).

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — False Data Injection Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef attacker fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B,color:#000
    classDef system fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000
    classDef defense fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#166534,color:#000

    nodeA1[Attacker Reconnaissance]:::attacker
    nodeA2[Craft Attack Vector a = Hc]:::attacker
    nodeA3[Inject y_a into Measurement Bus]:::attacker
    nodeS1[Measurement Channel y_k]:::system
    nodeS2[State Estimator x_hat]:::system
    nodeS3[Residual Based Detector]:::system
    nodeS4[Controller Command u_k]:::system
    nodeS5[Physical Plant x_dot = f x plus u]:::system
    nodeD1[Residual Comparison chi square]:::defense
    nodeD2[Robust Estimator]:::defense
    nodeD3[Anomaly Detection AI]:::defense
    nodeD4[Attack Blocked or Isolated]:::defense

    nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeA3 --> nodeS1 --> nodeS2 --> nodeS4 --> nodeS5
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3
    nodeD3 --> nodeD4
```

**Interpretation:** Maps the *attack kill chain* against the *defensive detection chain*. A KTU examiner's favorite block-level flow.

---

## 4.3 State Diagram — CPS Security State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> AnomalyDetected : IDS flag
    Normal --> UnderAttack : Active intrusion
    AnomalyDetected --> UnderAnalysis : SOC investigates
    UnderAnalysis --> Normal : False positive
    UnderAnalysis --> UnderAttack : Confirmed breach
    UnderAttack --> Containment : Activate isolation
    Containment --> Recovery : Re image and patch
    Recovery --> Normal : Restored and verified
    UnderAttack --> SafetyShutdown : Physical risk
    SafetyShutdown --> Recovery : Safe state achieved
```

**Interpretation:** Operational state transitions during a CPS cyber incident, including safety shutdown branch (uniquely CPS-specific).

---

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram — Privacy-Preserving CPS Data Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef sensor fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000
    classDef edge fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#075985,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#5B21B6,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000

    nodeP1[Smart Meter Sensors]:::sensor
    nodeP2[Wearable Health Devices]:::sensor
    nodeP3[Vehicle Telemetry Units]:::sensor
    nodeE1[Local Differential Privacy Layer]:::edge
    nodeE2[On device Anonymization]:::edge
    nodeE3[Encrypted Buffer and TPM]:::edge
    nodeC1[Secure Aggregation Server]:::cloud
    nodeC2[Homomorphic Computation]:::cloud
    nodeC3[Federated Learning Coordinator]:::cloud
    nodeU1[Authorized Analyst]:::user
    nodeU2[Auditor Read Only Access]:::user

    nodeP1 --> nodeE1
    nodeP2 --> nodeE2
    nodeP3 --> nodeE1
    nodeE1 --> nodeE3
    nodeE2 --> nodeE3
    nodeE3 --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2
    nodeC1 --> nodeC3
    nodeC2 --> nodeU1
    nodeC3 --> nodeU1
    nodeC1 --> nodeU2
```

**Interpretation:** Data flows from sensors through local privacy-preserving transformations (DP, anonymization, encryption) to a secure cloud where only aggregate or homomorphic results are exposed to authorized users. Auditor has read-only access. This is the canonical KTU privacy-engineering pipeline.
